title: Test bare drop/eat InventorySelector wiring
description:
## Context

Commands.Drop and Commands.Eat have two dispatch paths each:
- Bare ('drop', 'eat') — calls InventorySelector which hits terminal I/O, then redispatches to the indexed path
- Indexed ({'drop', '1'}, {'eat', '1'}) — calls gateway directly, straightforward to test

The bare path is currently untested at the command level. InventorySelector itself has its own unit tests covering its parsing and selection logic.

## Question to Resolve

Should the selector be made injectable (passed as a collaborator alongside gateway and message_buffer) or is it sufficient to test only the indexed dispatch paths and rely on InventorySelector's own tests for the selector behaviour?

## Suggested Approach

Make the selector injectable in the same pattern as gateway and message_buffer, allowing the bare drop/eat paths to be tested in isolation without terminal I/O.
notes: Indexed dispatch for Drop and Eat is now covered in the command unit tests added in bead 2se (drop_test.exs and eat_test.exs). Only the bare drop/eat paths remain untested here.

title: Crash player process on PlayerStore save failure
description:
## Problem

PlayerStore.save/2 return values are ignored throughout Player.ex handle_call implementations. If a DETS write fails (disk full, corrupted table, etc.), the player process continues with in-memory state that is not persisted to disk. The caller receives a success reply, and the mutation is silently lost on the next process crash or restart.

Example (current):
```elixir
{:ok, new_inventory} ->
  state = %{state | inventory: new_inventory}
  PlayerStore.save(state.id, state)  # return value ignored
  {:reply, :ok, state}
```

## Solution

Match on the PlayerStore.save/2 return value and crash the process on failure by returning an error tuple or raising. Let the supervisor handle recovery — on restart, Player.init/1 will reload the last successfully persisted state from DETS, which is honest about what was actually saved.

```elixir
{:ok, new_inventory} ->
  state = %{state | inventory: new_inventory}
  :ok = PlayerStore.save(state.id, state)  # crashes on failure
  {:reply, :ok, state}
```

## Rationale

This follows OTP convention: unexpected failures should crash the process and let the supervisor recover. Continuing with unpersisted state is worse than a clean restart — silent data loss is harder to debug and reason about than a visible process crash. The player loses the in-flight action but reconnects to their last good state.

title: Core combat resolution functions
description:
Pure functions in undercity_core for combat hit/miss and damage resolution. No OTP, no GenServer calls — just math.

Create a Combat module in undercity_core with:
1) hit?(weapon_stats) — roll hit/miss against base hit chance (e.g. 60-70%, could later factor in weapon hit_modifier from ome's weapon registry)
2) roll_damage(weapon_stats) — random damage within weapon's {damage_min, damage_max} range
3) resolve(weapon_stats) — combines hit? and roll_damage into a result tuple, e.g. {:hit, damage} | :miss

Input: weapon stats map from the weapon registry (ome). Output: a result tuple that the server layer can act on. Keep it simple — no player or process awareness, just pure combat math that's easy to unit test.

description: Define the full 5×5 grid layout and write all world content for the 16 new outer-ring cells. The existing 3×3 (Ashwell, North Alley, Wormgarden, West Street, Plaza, East Street, The Stray, South Alley, The Lame Horse) occupies positions (1,1)–(3,3) in the new grid, with Plaza remaining at the centre (2,2). The 16 new positions form the outer ring: row 0 (5 cells), row 4 (5 cells), col 0 rows 1–3 (3 cells), col 4 rows 1–3 (3 cells). At least one new exterior block must serve as the entrance to a new interior space (connected via :enter/:exit, like The Lame Horse → The Lame Horse Inn). All blocks must use existing types: :street, :square, :graveyard, :fountain, :space, :inn.
acceptance_criteria: 1. A grid map showing all 25 positions with block id, name, and type for every new cell. 2. A 2-sentence description for each new exterior block, conforming to STYLE_GUIDE.md. 3. At least one exterior/interior pair defined: the exterior block entry and the interior room, each with a name, type (:space / :inn), and description. 4. All type values are from the allowed set: :street, :square, :graveyard, :fountain, :space, :inn.
notes:
Content writer identified these possible locations in their initial investigation: The Charnel House, The Toll Gate, The Counting House, The Undercroft (interior), The Shambles, Bleachfield, The Gibbet Cross, The Sluice, The Catchpit, Quietside, The Bonehouse, The Narrows, The Breach, Gallows Reach. Names should follow the register of existing proper nouns — grounded, medievally plausible English.

IMPORTANT: One outer-ring cell must be designated as the Ashgate Tollbooth exterior. Use id: ashgate_tollbooth, name: 'Ashgate Tollbooth', type: :space. This block will receive an :enter exit to ashgate_tollbooth_interior in the Tollbooth epic (undercity-6yx), which is sequenced after this one. Ensure the exterior cell is placed and named correctly here so the Tollbooth epic can build on it.

title: Lazy-loaded Player GenServers
description: Discuss whether Player GenServers should be started on-demand and shut down after idle timeout rather than staying alive indefinitely. Currently players are started on connect and never stopped. Consider: idle timeout, restart-on-demand from DETS, atom table pressure from process naming, and cleanup of stale block memberships.
design:
## Implementation Plan

### Idle Timeout via GenServer :timeout

The only change needed now. Player GenServers stop after 15 minutes of inactivity.
On reconnect, Session.ensure_player_process already handles restarting the process
transparently — no changes needed there.

Block membership is unaffected — the player's ID stays in the Block's MapSet when
the GenServer shuts down. They remain in the world; the GenServer is just an
in-memory cache of their state.

File: apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/player.ex

- Add @idle_timeout_ms module attribute (configurable, 15 minutes to start)
- Update init/1 to return {:ok, state, @idle_timeout_ms}
- Add @idle_timeout_ms as third element to all handle_call reply tuples (auto-resets on activity)
- Add handle_info(:timeout, state) -> {:stop, :normal, state}

### Tests
File: apps/undercity_server/test/undercity_server/player_idle_test.exs (new)

- Player GenServer stops after idle timeout fires
- Reconnect via Session.ensure_player_process successfully restarts a stopped player

### Future: Atom Table and Distributed Process Naming

Current naming uses :player_#{id} (dynamic atoms, never GC'd). At current scale
this is a non-issue — player IDs are 16-char hex strings and the VM atom table
holds ~1 million entries by default.

When atom table growth becomes a real concern (hundreds of thousands of unique
players), the right solution is Horde:
- Horde.Registry — distributed registry, works across nodes, no dynamic atoms,
  auto-cleans entries on process death
- Horde.DynamicSupervisor — distributed supervisor for cross-node process management

The migration would be contained to via/1 in player.ex plus the supervision tree —
the rest of the codebase goes through via/1 and wouldn't need to change.

### Files
Mod: apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/player.ex
New: apps/undercity_server/test/undercity_server/player_idle_test.exs
close_reason: Idle timeout implemented with Player/Player.Server split and on-demand start via start_if_inactive

title: Refactor Commands dispatch to prevent god module growth
description:
## Problem

Commands.dispatch/5 is growing with every new command. Each clause follows the same skeleton (call Gateway → handle_action → render → return state) but with different success/error shapes and different render calls. Two related issues:

1. **God module risk** — every new command adds another clause here. With 7+ clauses already, this will become dense and hard to navigate.

2. **Implicit rendering contract** — there is no rule for which render functions to call after each action. Movement calls render_surroundings, render_description, and render_messages; eat only calls render_messages. This is implicit knowledge in each clause. Easy for a new command to miss a render call or render inconsistently.

3. **No tests** — Commands is the core integration point between user input and the server, but has no test coverage. It is tightly coupled to Gateway and View, making unit testing difficult in its current form.

## Goals

- Eliminate god module risk as command count grows
- Make the rendering contract explicit rather than implicit per-clause
- Promote ease of testing — the chosen pattern should make command handlers unit-testable in isolation without requiring full Gateway or View setup

## Questions to Answer Before Implementing

- How many commands do we expect long-term? A handful or significantly more?
- Should each command be its own module (e.g. Commands.Move, Commands.Search) with a shared behaviour/protocol? Or is a lighter abstraction sufficient?
- Should rendering responsibilities be encoded in a ViewRenderer or ActionRenderer module that maps action results to render calls? Or should commands declare what they render?
- Is the current pattern (each clause knows what to render) acceptable if we just document the convention, or does it need to be enforced structurally?

## Investigation

Explore patterns that address all three issues:
- Command handler modules with a shared behaviour
- A render-after-action mapping (action type → which blocks to update)
- A command registry approach (metadata-driven dispatch)

Weigh complexity of each against the actual expected command count, then implement the chosen approach and add tests.
notes:
## Remaining Work

### Step 3: Unit tests for individual command modules

The structural refactoring (steps 1 and 2) is complete and committed. What remains is unit tests for each command module in isolation.

**What's in place:**
- FakeGateway, ExhaustedGateway, CollapsedGateway and FakeMessageBuffer are defined in commands_test.exs. These should be extracted to a test support file (e.g. test/support/fakes.ex) before adding more test files that need them.
- The integration test covers routing and cross-cutting concerns (exhaustion, collapse, unknown input).
- Command module unit tests should cover the specific success/error branches per command without duplicating the exhaustion/collapse cases already covered.

**Commands to test and key branches:**
- Commands.Move: successful move ({:moved, new_state}), no exit ({:continue with warning})
- Commands.Search: found item, found but full, nothing
- Commands.Inventory: empty inventory, items present
- Commands.Drop: indexed dispatch — success, invalid index; bare drop is deferred (see bead undercity-1ll)
- Commands.Eat: indexed dispatch — success, not edible, invalid index; bare eat deferred (see undercity-1ll)
- Commands.Scribble: bare scribble (usage warning), with text — success, empty message, no chalk

**Note on Scribble:**
The success and empty_message branches call BlockDescription.scribble_surface(vicinity), which requires a real %UndercityServer.Vicinity{} struct. Use %Vicinity{type: :street} or similar in test state for those cases.

title: Commands can describe their own usage
description: Each command module exposes a usage/0 function. The router aggregates these to auto-generate the unknown command warning and power a future help command, replacing the manually maintained hint string.
design:
## Implementation Plan

### 1. Add usage/0 to each command module
Each command module exposes a public usage/0 returning a concise syntax string:
- Commands.Search     — "search"
- Commands.Inventory  — "inventory (or i)"
- Commands.Drop       — "drop [n]"
- Commands.Eat        — "eat [n]"
- Commands.Scribble   — "scribble <text>"
- Commands.Move       — "move: north, south, east, west (or n, s, e, w), enter, exit"
- Commands.Help       — "help"

### 2. Add usage_hints/0 to Commands router
Private helper in Commands that maps over @command_routes, calls module.usage() on each,
deduplicates, sorts alphabetically, and joins with newlines or commas for display.

### 3. Add a help command
When the player types 'help', display the aggregated usage hints from all command modules.
This is the primary entry point for players to discover available commands.

### 4. Update the unknown command message
Replace the hardcoded hint string with a short prompt:
  "Unknown command. Type 'help' for a list of commands."

### 5. No @behaviour needed
Convention is sufficient — usage/0 is a simple public function. Formal behaviour would
be overkill for a single zero-argument function.

### Files
- apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/commands.ex — add usage_hints/0, update unknown command message, add help to @command_routes
- apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/commands/help.ex — new Help command module
- apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/commands/{search,inventory,drop,eat,scribble,move}.ex — add def usage/0

## Open Questions

**Should Help be a full command module or handled inline in the router?**
A full Commands.Help module is consistent with the existing pattern and keeps the router
clean. Handling it inline would be simpler but breaks the convention. Decide when picking
this up — either is fine given the small scope.

title: Investigate :global.sync() usage and alternatives
description: Server.connect/2 currently calls :global.sync() after Node.connect to ensure global name registration is synchronized before the GenServer.call. This works but is a blocking call that waits for all nodes to sync. Investigate whether this is the best approach or if there are better alternatives (e.g. retrying the GenServer.call, using :global.whereis_name with a poll, or restructuring to avoid the race). Also consider implications as cluster size grows — :global.sync() could become slow with many nodes.
notes:
Replaced :global.sync() with RPC-based approach:

- Server now registers with local Registry instead of :global
- connect/2 checks local Registry first (for tests/same-node), falls back to RPC
- No changes needed to CLI commands or mix tasks
- Trade-off: loses automatic process migration if server moves nodes, but acceptable for current single-server architecture
close_reason: Replaced :global.sync() with local Registry + RPC approach. Changes committed to branch 2wt-rpc-connect.

title: Generic item-usage system (use <item> <args>)
description:
Scribble is currently a standalone command with its own Gateway function, but it's really just 'use Chalk with some text.' As more usable items are added, we don't want a bespoke Gateway function and CLI command for each one.

Refactor towards a generic item-usage pattern: `use <item> <args>` — where the system verifies the player has the item, then dispatches to item-specific behaviour with the given args. From the client/view side, commands can still have friendly names (e.g. 'scribble' maps to 'use Chalk'), but the backend should route through a single unified path.

**Key design goal:** Adding a new usable item should not require changes to Gateway. Items should define their own behaviour, and the use-item pipeline should discover and dispatch to it.

**Investigation / questions to resolve:**
- Where does item behaviour live? On the item definition (UndercityCore.Item)? A behaviour/protocol? A registry of item handlers?
- How are item-specific args validated? Each item type may need different args (Chalk needs text, a Key might need a door ID, etc.)
- Should items declare what args they expect, or is validation purely in the handler?
- How does this interact with the Block process? Scribble mutates block state — other items might too (e.g. a torch lighting a dark room). Is there a generic 'apply item effect to block' callback?
- What's the client contract? Does the CLI send `use Chalk hello world` and the view layer parses it, or does the client send structured data?
- How do we handle items with passive effects vs active use (if that distinction matters yet)?
- Review existing Item and Inventory modules in UndercityCore to understand what extension points already exist
notes:
## Design Decisions (from discussion)

### Item behaviour dispatch
- Handler modules by convention: Items.Chalk, Items.Key, etc.
- Item struct stays as-is (plain %Item{name, uses})
- Dispatch via Module.concat(Items, item_name) + function_exported?/3
- No @behaviour or compile-time registry — tests cover each item handler
- Adding a new usable item = drop a new module in Items.*, no Gateway changes

## Completed Prerequisites (3ac)

### Player.use_item/3 — atomic AP + item consumption
- Single GenServer call: spends AP and consumes item atomically (apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/player.ex)
- Returns {:ok, ap} on success, {:error, :exhausted} or {:error, :item_missing} on failure
- consume_item/2 private helper handles inventory find/use/update logic
- Player.use_item/2 (without cost) also exists, returns :ok or :not_found

### Scribble refactored as reference pattern (apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/actions/scribble.ex)
- Uses Player.use_item/3 with a `with` passthrough — errors flow through automatically
- Sanitises text first, then calls use_item, then calls Block.scribble on success
- Contract: {:ok, ap}, {:error, :empty_message}, {:error, :item_missing}, {:error, :exhausted}
- CLI handles :empty_message as visual no-op (shows scribble message but no AP/chalk consumed)
- This is the pattern to generalise: validate args -> use_item -> apply effect

### Gateway contract pattern
- Gateway.scribble delegates to Actions.Scribble — this delegation should become generic use_item dispatch
- Success returns {:ok, ap}, failures return {:error, reason} — no AP in error cases
- CLI keeps its own AP state and only updates on success

## Remaining Questions for 2xd
- Where do item handler modules live? Suggest UndercityCore.Items.Chalk or UndercityServer.Items.Chalk
- Handler execute/2 signature: what context does it receive? Likely needs at minimum block_id
- How does the CLI map friendly commands (scribble) to use_item calls? Parse layer or a registry?
- Should Gateway expose a single use_item/3 or keep friendly delegations that route internally?

## Related Beads
- 5a3: Eliminate get_ap server call from Player (not blocking, but relevant to contract cleanup)

title: Allow players to heal themselves and others using a consumable item
description: Add a healing item that players can use to restore HP to themselves or another player in the same block. The item is consumed on use and has a defined heal amount. This covers the domain model, server action, CLI command, and player-facing feedback.

title: Define the Salve item in the domain layer
description:
Add UndercityCore.Remedy — a registry module mapping healing item names to heal amounts, following the exact pattern of UndercityCore.Food. Add the Salve item to UndercityCore.LootTable (inn block type, single-use).

The Remedy module should expose:
- Remedy.effect(item_name) :: {:heal, pos_integer()} | :not_a_remedy
- Remedy.remedy?(item_name) :: boolean()

The initial entry: "Salve" => {:heal, 15}
acceptance_criteria:
- UndercityCore.Remedy module exists and maps "Salve" to {:heal, 15}
- Remedy.effect/1 returns {:heal, 15} for "Salve" and :not_a_remedy for unknown names
- Remedy.remedy?/1 returns true for "Salve", false otherwise
- LootTable includes a single-use Salve entry for the inn block type
- All existing tests pass, new unit tests cover Remedy lookups and the LootTable entry
notes: Follow UndercityCore.Food and UndercityCore.Combat.Weapon as the pattern. The heal amount (15) is intentional — 30% of max HP (50). Do not add a :description field to Item; that is out of scope for this bead.

title: Add server-side Salve healing action
acceptance_criteria:
- heal/4 with self-target consumes the Salve and restores HP atomically (no AP spent or item consumed on failure)
- heal/4 with other-target consumes the Salve and calls take_heal on the target
- No item or AP consumed when target is at full HP (:already_full)
- No item or AP consumed when target is collapsed (:collapsed)
- No item or AP consumed when actor does not have a Salve (:item_missing)
- Self-targeting does not deadlock (uses atomic callback, not Player.perform + take_heal)
- take_heal is symmetric to take_damage: guards at 0 HP, clamps at max HP
- All existing tests pass; new tests cover self-heal, other-heal, full-HP, collapsed, and missing-item cases
notes:
IMPORTANT — avoid deadlock: self-heal must NOT use Player.perform/3 followed by take_heal on the same process. Player.perform/3 makes a :spend_ap call to the actor's GenServer, then runs the action fn. If the action fn then calls take_heal on the same GenServer, it will deadlock (GenServer cannot receive a new call while handling one). The atomic self-heal callback (handle_call {:self_heal, ...}) avoids this entirely.

The notification to the target is blocked on undercity-w6m. Add a comment/stub in the action module at the point where the notification would be sent.

title: Add the use command to the CLI
description:
Implement the Commands.Use module and register the 'use' verb, allowing players to use a Salve on themselves or another player in the same block.

Command syntax:
  use              — InventorySelector ('Use which item?') → TargetSelector ('Use on who?')
  use <n>          — skips InventorySelector, opens TargetSelector
  use <n> <target> — direct dispatch (no selectors)

Target selection:
- state.vicinity.people already includes the current player (no structural changes to GameState or Vicinity needed)
- Pass state.vicinity.people directly to TargetSelector — the player can select themselves or others
- When target came from TargetSelector, use target.id directly (skip find_target_id lookup)
- When target is typed by name in 'use <n> <target>', use find_target_id as in attack

Argument order is item-first: <n> <target>. Do not reuse attack's parse_rest/1 — write a use-specific parser.

Use dispatch/5 arity with injected default selectors (same pattern as attack) to support Mimic mocking in tests.

Pass state.player_id as target_id when the player selects themselves.

Result messages (all from content-writer, treat as final):
- Self-heal success:   'You press the salve into the wound. It holds.'
- Other-heal success:  'You dress {Name}s wound. Theyll live a while longer.'
- Already full HP (self):  'Your wounds have already closed. You save the salve.'
- Already full HP (other): '{Name} has no wounds to dress.'
- Target not here:     '{Name} is not here.'
- No Salve in inventory: 'You reach for the salve. Theres nothing there.'
- Collapsed target:    '{Name} cannot be helped.'
- :exhausted / :collapsed / :not_in_block are handled by Commands.handle_action/4 uniformly

Use GameState.continue(state, new_ap, new_hp) for successful heals; the game loop's Constitution.threshold_messages handles tier-crossing health messages automatically.
acceptance_criteria:
- 'use' verb registered in @command_routes
- All three input forms (bare, with index, with index and target) work correctly
- TargetSelector includes the current player as a valid target
- All result messages match the content-writer copy above

No change needed to TargetSelector itself. If you feel the empty-state message is misleading, it can be updated as a minor improvement in this bead.

title: Configure Dialyzer for static type checking
Atomic AP + item consumption in Player GenServer
description:
Player.perform/3 and Player.use_item/2 are two sequential GenServer calls to the same Player process. For item-consuming actions (like scribble), this means:

1. Player.perform -> {:spend_ap, cost} (AP deducted)
2. Player.use_item -> {:use_item, "Chalk"} (inventory checked)

This has two problems:
- Double round-trip to the same process
- AP is spent even if the item is missing (inconsistency)

## Solution

Add a single GenServer callback that atomically checks AP and consumes the item:

```elixir
def handle_call({:use_item, item_name, cost}, _from, state) do
  with {:ok, ap_state} <- ActionPoints.spend(state.action_points, cost),
       {:ok, inventory, item} <- consume_item(state.inventory, item_name) do
    state = %{state | action_points: ap_state, inventory: inventory}
    save(state)
    {:reply, {:ok, item, ap_state.current}, state}
  else
    {:error, :exhausted} -> {:reply, {:error, :exhausted}, state}
    {:error, :not_found} -> {:reply, {:error, :not_found}, state}
  end
end
```

This gives item-usage actions a dedicated path separate from perform/3 (which remains for plain AP actions like move and search).

## Context

This is a prerequisite for the generic item-usage system (2xd). The use-item pipeline will route through this atomic call, then dispatch to the item handler's effect.
close_reason: Implemented on branch 3ac-atomic-use-item. Player.use_item/3 provides atomic AP+item consumption. Scribble refactored to use it.

title: Full inventory: drop action, search behaviour, and max size
Action points system
description:
Players have 50 action points (AP) that gate actions and regenerate over time.

Core mechanics:
- Players spawn with 50 AP
- AP cannot go below 0
- Moving, searching, and scribbling each cost 1 AP (future actions will also cost AP, some variable, but out of scope)
- Players cannot perform actions when they don't have enough AP
- AP regenerates at 1 point per 30 minutes when below 50

Awareness messaging (not a numeric display):
- 40+ points: "you feel rested"
- 25 points: "you feel weary"
- 10 points: "you are starting to feel exhausted"
- 0 points: "you are exhausted"
- After performing an action, if a threshold is crossed (excluding the 40+ one), the message is shown to the player in the next render with a color highlight
- Also shown on reconnect
notes:
This may need refining and possibly breaking up into sub-tasks once we start working on it.

Blocked by cid (Scribbling) because we want to build the action system on the actions introduced in that bead.

Blocked by 1gr (Searching) because it introduces a new action (searching) that AP should apply to, and it also introduces a process/store for player state that we can build on to persist AP.

notes:
Stale DETS data caused crashes when the Block struct changed (added exits field). Persisted blocks without the new field caused KeyError on list_exits. Need to consider schema migration or defensive loading when deserializing from DETS.

Consider DETS file per block instead of a single store — keeps blocks fully independent, avoids single file bottleneck, and a corrupt file only affects one block. Scales to 10k+ blocks.

title: Implement player movement
description: Handle moving a person from one block to another. Needs to remove from current block, add to destination, and validate the exit exists. Likely lives in Gateway or a new module.
design:
## Future: Write-ahead move log (post-MVP)

To guarantee no lost players AND no duplicates, each block maintains its own move intent log in its DETS store.

### Happy path
1. Block A writes intent: {moving_out, player, destination: B}
2. Block A removes player from people, saves
3. Block B adds player, saves
4. Block A deletes the intent

### Crash recovery
A temporary Task child in BlockSupervisor runs after Block starts. It reads pending intents from Store and resolves them:
- If player still in A: remove from A, join to B, delete intent
- If player not in A but not in B: join to B, delete intent
- If player already in B: delete intent (move completed)
- If no pending intents: exit immediately

Task is restart: :temporary under :rest_for_one, so it re-runs if Block crashes (correct behavior since a crash could leave a new incomplete move). In most cases it early-exits with no work.

### Supervision tree
BlockSupervisor (:rest_for_one)
  Store -> Block -> RepairTask (temporary)

### Blocking player actions during move
Players with an active move intent must not be able to submit actions. Block A should reject commands for a player that has a pending moving_out intent. This prevents interleaving actions with an in-progress move.

### Retry concern
If destination block B is down during repair, the task needs to retry. handle_continue or periodic retry until B is available.
notes: ## Movement consistency (MVP)\n\nLeave-first ordering: remove from source block, then join destination. Guarantees no duplicate players. Accepts the risk of a lost player if crash occurs between leave and join — player can reconnect via enter/1 to respawn. At the DB level, each DETS save is atomic per-file, so the invariant (player in at most one block) holds on disk too.

title: Validate Ratatouille/termbox compiles in POSIX environment
description:
## Context

Investigated Ratatouille (https://github.com/ndreynolds/ratatouille) as a replacement for Owl to implement the two-column layout required by lz3.

## Why Ratatouille?

Owl.LiveScreen blocks are strictly vertical and stacked — there is no Owl API for side-by-side regions. The two-column layout in lz3 would require manually composing iodata (zipping lines from left and right panels) inside a single Owl block.

Ratatouille uses a 12-column grid DSL (row/column elements, like HTML) where two-column layout is trivial and responsive to terminal width. It also uses the Elm Architecture (TEA: init/update/render) which maps cleanly onto our GameState model.

## Why it blocked on Windows

Ratatouille depends on ex_termbox, which uses a C NIF (native code). On Windows/MSYS2/Git Bash, mix compile fails immediately:

  ** (Mix) 'nmake' not found in the path.

Even if the NIF compiled, termbox requires POSIX terminal APIs (termios, tcsetattr) for raw mode — these don't exist in MSYS2/Git Bash.

## Resolution path

Run the client from a POSIX environment. WSL2 provides a real Linux kernel on Windows. Once WSL2 is set up:

1. Clone the repo inside the WSL2 filesystem (~/work/undercity) — NOT via /mnt/c/ (cross-filesystem I/O is slow)
2. Install Erlang/Elixir in WSL2
3. Run: mix deps.get && mix compile — verify ex_termbox builds cleanly
4. If it compiles, run the rendering.exs example from the Ratatouille repo to confirm the terminal renders correctly

Only if that all succeeds should we proceed with the Ratatouille migration for lz3.

## Transferability summary (if termbox works)

- GameState struct → TEA model (near 1:1)
- Commands routing/modules → reusable, return model updates instead of pushing to buffer
- MessageBuffer → becomes a list field in model (no Agent needed, simpler)
- View.BlockDescription, View.Status → logic reusable, output format changes to DSL elements
- View.Surroundings → rework from Owl.Table to Ratatouille table/panel DSL; hardcoded @cell_width goes away
- Input module (IO.gets) → DELETED, replaced by keypress accumulation in model (editor.exs pattern)
- InventorySelector/TargetSelector → significant rework: blocking receive → explicit mode in model state machine
- Two-column layout → trivial: row do column(size: 9) ... column(size: 3) ... end
- Persistent message log → easy: list field in model, render the tail
close_reason: Created in error — replaced by correct bead covering full Owl→Ratatouille migration

title: Reduce WorldMap churn: BlockType module and derived grid bounds
description:
## Summary

Two small structural changes to reduce the number of files touched when the world grows.

Identified via /survey: world_map.ex has a 1.89:1 add:delete ratio and was touched in 13 of the last 20 commits. The churn comes from two sources: type metadata scattered across files, and hardcoded grid bounds that must be kept in sync with grid data by hand.

---

## Change 1: Extract `UndercityCore.BlockType` module

### Problem

Adding a new block type currently touches 3 files:
- `world_map.ex` — add the type atom to `@block_defs`
- `block.ex` — extend the `block_type()` typespec union
- `block_description.ex` (CLI) — add entries to `@descriptions`, possibly new `scribble_surface` and `block_prefix` clauses

Nothing enforces these stay in sync. A missing description key renders silently as nil.

### Solution

Create `apps/undercity_core/lib/undercity_core/block_type.ex` that owns:
- The `t()` typespec (the union of all block type atoms)
- `description/1` — one clause per type atom (and compound `{:space, building_type}` keys)
- `scribble_surface/1` — one clause per type atom
- `block_prefix/1` — one clause per type atom

Update `block.ex` to reference `BlockType.t()` instead of defining its own union.

Update `block_description.ex` to call `BlockType.description/1`, `BlockType.scribble_surface/1`, `BlockType.block_prefix/1` instead of holding its own maps and pattern-match clauses.

Note: the description strings are world content, not presentation — moving them to core is correct. The CLI keeps the rendering assembly (Owl tags, colours, interpolation).

### After this change

| What you're doing | Files touched |
|---|---|
| Adding a new location (existing type) | `world_map.ex` only |
| Inventing a new kind of place | `world_map.ex` + `block_type.ex` |

### Example: adding a :market building type

Before (3 files):
- world_map.ex: add block defs + connection
- block.ex: add `:market` to typespec union
- block_description.ex: add `market:` and `space_market:` description entries, new `scribble_surface` clause

After (2 files):
- world_map.ex: add block defs + connection (unchanged)
- block_type.ex: add `:market` to typespec, add `description(:market)`, `description({:space, :market})`, `scribble_surface(:market)` clauses

---

## Change 2: Derive grid bounds from grid data

### Problem

`grid_cell/2` in `world_map.ex` has hardcoded bounds:

```elixir
defp grid_cell(r, c) when r >= 0 and r < 3 and c >= 0 and c < 3 do
```

Adding a new row to `@grid` requires a separate manual edit to this guard. The data and the constraint on it are separate expressions with no link.

### Solution

Derive the bounds at compile time from the grid itself:

```elixir
@grid_rows length(@grid)
@grid_cols @grid |> hd() |> length()

defp grid_cell(r, c) when r >= 0 and r < @grid_rows and c >= 0 and c < @grid_cols do
  @grid |> Enum.at(r) |> Enum.at(c)
end
```

### After this change

Expanding the world grid (adding rows/columns to `@grid`) only requires the data edit. The bounds update automatically. No separate guard change to remember.

title: Eliminate get_ap server call from Player
description:
Player.get_ap/1 is a GenServer.call that exists mainly to read AP for return values. Consider eliminating it or reducing its usage.

## Current usages

1. **Session.enter/1 (new player, line 75):** Calls get_ap right after creating the player with ActionPoints.new() — we already know it's 50. Redundant.

2. **Session.enter/1 (reconnect, line 58):** Calls get_ap after ensure_player_process. This is the one case where we genuinely need the current AP. Discuss: could Session return AP from the player process startup/reconnect flow instead of a separate call? Or could the CLI fetch AP lazily?

3. **Gateway.get_ap/1 (line 19):** Exposed as a gateway function. Check if anything still calls this after the scribble refactor in 3ac.

## Goal

Reduce unnecessary round-trips to the Player GenServer. The AP value should flow naturally from operations that already touch the Player process, rather than requiring a separate call.

title: Improve codebase discoverability for AI agents
description:
## Motivation

With fresh context, Claude (and other agents) start by investigating the project — globbing, grepping, and manually parsing source files to understand the architecture. This is time-consuming, expensive, and repeats every session or after context compaction.

We should add upfront documentation that lets agents orient quickly without needing to parse code.

## Scope

1. **Identify common agent exploration patterns** — before brainstorming solutions, audit what agents actually do when they first encounter this codebase (e.g. globbing app directories, reading mix.exs files, tracing GenServer trees, grepping for module definitions). This keeps us solving real problems rather than guessing.

2. **Brainstorm improvements** for agent discoverability:
   - What documents are missing? (e.g. ARCHITECTURE.md, module/app summaries)
   - What structural info would help? (umbrella app responsibilities, key modules, data flow, domain concepts)
   - How should CLAUDE.md be updated to point agents to these docs so they don't start by reading code?
   - What common tasks cheat sheets would help? (e.g. to add a new command look at X, to add a new block type see Y)

3. **Implement the best options**, likely including:
   - An ARCHITECTURE.md or similar doc covering:
     - Umbrella app breakdown (undercity_core, undercity_server, undercity_cli) and their responsibilities
     - Key modules and their roles
     - Data flow / how the system fits together
     - Domain concepts and terminology
   - Project structure overview (directory layout, where to find things)
   - Update CLAUDE.md with pointers to architecture docs and a start here section for agents
   - Consider a module-level summary index if the codebase warrants it

4. **Keep it lightweight** — the goal is orientation, not exhaustive documentation. Enough that an agent can skip the parse everything phase and jump to relevant code.

## Notes

- Target audience is AI agents (Claude Code, Copilot, etc.), not just humans
- Docs should be low-maintenance — describe stable architecture, not implementation details that churn
- Consider what info would have saved the most exploration time in past sessions
design:
## Implementation Plan

### 1. Create docs/ folder with the following files

docs/architecture.md
- Umbrella overview: three apps (core/server/cli) and their responsibilities
- Key modules per app with 1-2 line descriptions
- Data flow: CLI input → Commands → Gateway → Actions → GenServer → DETS → reply

docs/domain.md
- Core concepts: Block, Player, AP, Health, Vicinity, WorldMap, LootTable
- Terminology and how concepts relate to each other

docs/commands-and-actions.md
- How to add a CLI command (Commands module, @command_routes, dispatch pattern)
- How to add a server-side action (Actions module, Gateway dispatch, AP cost pattern)
- End-to-end: how a command maps to an action maps to a GenServer call

docs/persistence.md
- DETS model: per-player store, per-block store
- Where data lives on disk
- How to reset with mix reset (no prod data to worry about)

docs/testing.md
- Test.Helpers usage: start_block!, start_player!
- Where unit tests vs integration tests live (core vs server)
- Async patterns and DETS cleanup

### 2. Update CLAUDE.md
- Add a 'Documentation' section near the top pointing agents to docs/ as the first stop
- Brief description of what each doc covers so agents know which one to reach for

### 3. Second pass
Once the initial docs are written, do a brainstorming pass:
- What questions came up during the session that the docs didn't answer?
- Are there any missing topics (e.g. supervision tree, session lifecycle, block types)?
- Add any new docs or sections that would have saved exploration time

### Files
New:  docs/architecture.md
New:  docs/domain.md
New:  docs/commands-and-actions.md
New:  docs/persistence.md
New:  docs/testing.md
Mod:  CLAUDE.md

title: Server: heal action, Player receive_heal callback, Gateway routing
description:
Wire the healing item into the server action pipeline.

## What to build

### 1. Player.Server — new GenServer callback
Add handle_call({:receive_heal, amount}) to UndercityServer.Player.Server:
- Calls Player.receive_heal(state.player, amount) (pure core function from Core bead)
- Updates state.player, calls save!()
- Replies {:ok, Health.current(new_health)} — just the new HP value
- No AP cost, no collapse guard (healing a collapsed player is valid)

### 2. Player facade — new public function
Add to UndercityServer.Player:
  def receive_heal(player_id, amount) do
    PlayerServer.call(player_id, PlayerSupervisor, {:receive_heal, amount})
  end
Returns {:ok, non_neg_integer()}

### 3. Player.Server — new GenServer callback for self-heal
Add handle_call({:use_remedy, index}) mirroring {:eat_item, index}:
- Calls Player.use_remedy(state.player, index, now())
- On {:ok, player, item, effect}: save!(), reply {:ok, item, effect, ActionPoints.current(ap), Health.current(hp)}
- On error: reply the error unchanged

### 4. Player facade — use_remedy/2
  def use_remedy(player_id, index) do
    PlayerServer.call(player_id, PlayerSupervisor, {:use_remedy, index})
  end

### 5. Actions.Heal module
Create apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/actions/heal.ex:

  def heal(player_id, block_id, target_id, item_index) when player_id == target_id do
    # self-heal: consume item and apply heal to self
    Player.use_remedy(player_id, item_index)
  end

  def heal(player_id, block_id, target_id, item_index) do
    with :ok <- validate_target(player_id, block_id, target_id),
         {:ok, item} <- find_remedy(player_id, item_index),
         heal_amount <- Remedy.effect(item.name) do  # {:heal, N}
      Player.perform(player_id, fn ->
        Player.use_item(player_id, item.name)  # consume item
        {:heal, n} = heal_amount
        {:ok, _hp} = Player.receive_heal(target_id, n)
        {:healed, target_id, item.name, n}
      end)
    end
  end

NOTE: For healing others, item consumption and AP spend must both happen atomically via Player.perform. The action function should consume the item from the healer's inventory (Player.use_item/2) and apply the heal to the target.

Alternatively, model it as: validate target -> find remedy item -> Player.perform(player_id, fn -> consume item + receive_heal on target). Use whichever approach is cleanest — see Actions.Attack for reference.

### 6. Gateway routing
Add to Gateway:
  def perform(player_id, block_id, :heal, {target_id, item_index}) do
    if Block.has_person?(block_id, player_id) do
      Actions.Heal.heal(player_id, block_id, target_id, item_index)
    else
      {:error, :not_in_block}
    end
  end

Or add a dispatch clause if restructuring perform is cleaner. Allow self-targeting (player_id == target_id), which skips the block membership check for target.

## Return shapes

Self-heal: {:ok, item, {:heal, N}, remaining_ap, new_hp}
Heal other: {:ok, {:healed, target_id, item_name, N}, remaining_ap}
Errors: {:error, :invalid_target} | {:error, :not_a_remedy} | {:error, :invalid_index} | {:error, :exhausted} | {:error, :collapsed}

## Constraints

- Target must be in the same block (block_id check), unless target == player_id
- Cannot heal with a non-remedy item — validate via Remedy.remedy?/1 before consuming
- Item is consumed on use regardless of outcome (the item is spent on the attempt)
- All mutations must be persisted via save!() before replying

## Acceptance criteria

- Player.receive_heal/2 adds HP to a player and persists
- Player.receive_heal/2 on a player at max HP clamps to max (no overflow)
- Player.receive_heal/2 on a collapsed player (0 HP) raises HP — healing the fallen is valid
- Actions.Heal.heal(self, ...) applies heal to the acting player and consumes the item
- Actions.Heal.heal(other, ...) consumes item from healer, applies HP to target
- Gateway :heal returns {:error, :not_in_block} when player is not in block
- {:error, :invalid_target} when target is not in same block
- {:error, :not_a_remedy} when the selected item is not a remedy
- Server integration test: two players in same block, one heals the other, target HP increases

title: Extract block_info into a struct (Bubble or similar)
description: Define a struct in UndercityServer (or Core) to wrap the block_info map returned by Block.info/1. This struct would own derived helpers like inside?/1 and grid_centre/1 that currently live as private View helpers.
notes:
DECISIONS SUMMARY:

Struct: UndercityServer.Vicinity - represents what the player perceives from where they stand. A limited window of the world centred on the player's position that moves with them.

Vicinity fields/functions:
- neighbourhood: 3x3 grid of block ids (from WorldMap.surrounding/2)
- people: list of Person structs (runtime, from GenServer)
- id/1: current block id, extracted from neighbourhood[1][1]
- name/1: display name with building type suffix if applicable (e.g. 'The Lame Horse Inn'). Delegates to Core.
- inside?/1: is the player at a building entrance. Delegates to Core.
- building?/2: is a given block id a building. Delegates to Core.

Core changes (WorldMap):
- Add surrounding/2: returns 3x3 grid of block ids around given block id. Has distance param (default 3). Replaces block_context/1.
- Add block_name/1: public accessor for display name by block id.
- Add block_type/1: public accessor for block type atom by block id.
- building_type/1: no change, already public.
- Remove building_names/0: no longer needed (building? uses building_type instead).
- Remove block_context/1: replaced by surrounding/2.

Key principles:
- CLI talks only to Server (Vicinity), not Core
- Server delegates domain logic to Core internally
- description_key and prefix stay in CLI (presentation-specific)
- name/1 always includes building type suffix (no separate display_name)

title: Screen.read_input/0 can hang indefinitely if IO.gets stalls
description:
## Problem

Screen.read_input/0 spawns a Task and blocks on receive with no timeout:

```elixir
Task.start(fn ->
  input = IO.gets("")
  send(caller, {:input, input})
end)

receive do
  {:input, input} -> input
end
```

If IO.gets hangs or the task crashes, the receive never unblocks and the CLI freezes permanently with no recovery path.

## Fix

Add an after clause to the receive, or switch to Task.async/Task.await with a timeout:

```elixir
receive do
  {:input, input} -> input
after
  30_000 -> :timeout
end
```

The caller (GameLoop) should handle the :timeout return and loop back to waiting for input.
design:
## Implementation Plan

### 1. Screen.read_input/0
File: apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/view/screen.ex (lines 68-80)

Store the task reference and add an after clause:

  def read_input do
    caller = self()
    {:ok, task} = Task.start(fn ->
      input = IO.gets("")
      send(caller, {:input, input})
    end)

    receive do
      {:input, input} -> input
    after
      60_000 -> {:timeout, task}
    end
  end

Returns {:timeout, task} so the caller can kill the hung process.

### 2. GameLoop
File: apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/game_loop.ex

Pattern match on read_input/0 result. On timeout, kill the task and loop back silently
— no message to the player. They can just type again as normal.

  case View.read_input() do
    {:timeout, task} ->
      Task.shutdown(task, :kill)
      loop(player, state)

    input when is_binary(input) ->
      # existing dispatch logic
  end

No warning message shown to the player. The timeout is purely a defensive recovery
mechanism for genuine IO stalls (broken pipe, OS-level hang). At 60 seconds, normal
players will never encounter it.

title: Document branching convention in CLAUDE.md
description: Add branching convention to CLAUDE.md. Branches should be named based on the beads task ID plus a descriptor, e.g. undercity-shm-scaffold-umbrella. Document this so it's followed consistently.

title: Discuss undercity concept and plan initial steps
notes:
## Concept Summary

- **Genre**: Multiplayer persistent text game (browser + CLI)
- **Setting**: Dark medieval world, zombie apocalypse. Tone of Berserk / Between Two Fires — grim, grounded, not heroic
- **Players**: Survivors with open-ended roles (trade, steal, help, fight, hide — DayZ style emergent gameplay)
- **Undead**: Ever-present environmental threat, dumb but dangerous. Not the strategic focus

title: CLI: heal command dispatch and rendering
description:
Implement the CLI heal command following the attack command as the primary pattern.

## What to build

Create apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/commands/heal.ex implementing UndercityCli.Commands.Heal.

### Command surface

Verb: 'heal'

Three dispatch forms (same pattern as attack):
  1. 'heal'            -> inventory selector ('Heal with what?') -> target selector ('Heal who?') -> dispatch
  2. 'heal <target>'   -> inventory selector -> dispatch
  3. 'heal <target> <n>' -> dispatch directly (n is 1-based item index)

Self-targeting: 'heal me' or 'heal self' or the player's own name should be treated as self-heal (target_id == player_id). This skips the target-present-in-block check on the server side and is always valid.

Alternatively, allow bare 'heal <n>' (no target = self-heal with item n). Either is acceptable — pick the approach that gives the cleanest dispatch branching.

### Target selector behaviour

Reuse UndercityCli.View.TargetSelector (already exists).
People list from state.vicinity.people (same as attack).
Include self in the people list so the player can target themselves interactively. When no other players are present, the selector should still offer self as an option (or fall through to self-heal directly).

### Inventory selector

Reuse UndercityCli.View.InventorySelector (already exists).
Label: 'Heal with what?'

### Gateway call

gateway.perform(player_id, vicinity.id, :heal, {target_id, item_index})

### handle_outcome clauses

Success (self-heal):
  {:ok, item, {:heal, n}, new_ap, new_hp} ->
    message_buffer.success('[flavour text for self-heal — from content bead]')
    GameState.continue(state, new_ap, new_hp)

Success (heal other):
  {:ok, {:healed, target_id, item_name, n}, new_ap} ->
    # look up target name from state.vicinity.people
    message_buffer.success('[flavour text for heal-other — from content bead]')
    # HP hasn't changed for the acting player
    GameState.continue(state, new_ap, state.hp)

Error cases:
  {:error, :invalid_target}  -> message_buffer.warn('[target not found message]')
  {:error, :not_a_remedy}   -> message_buffer.warn('[cannot heal with that message]')
  {:error, :invalid_index}   -> message_buffer.warn('Invalid item selection.')
  :cancel                    -> GameState.continue(state)

Exhausted/collapsed are handled upstream by Commands.handle_action/4 automatically.

### usage/0

Return a concise string, e.g.: 'heal [target] [n]'

### Verb registration

Add to @command_routes in Commands:
  {UndercityCli.Commands.Heal, ["heal"]}

## Constraints

- CLI must not reference UndercityCore types directly — work only with the shapes returned by Gateway
- All messages use the exact flavour text agreed in the Content bead
- Self-heal result shape is {:ok, item, {:heal, n}, new_ap, new_hp} (same as eat result shape — item is a map/struct with .name)
- Heal-other result shape is {:ok, {:healed, target_id, item_name, n}, new_ap}

## Acceptance criteria

- 'heal' with no args launches inventory selector then target selector
- 'heal <name>' launches inventory selector then dispatches
- 'heal <name> <n>' dispatches directly
- Self-targeting works (player can heal themselves)
- All success/error outcomes render correct messages
- Commands.usage_hints/0 includes the heal usage string
- Tests use Mimic stubs for Gateway, MessageBuffer, InventorySelector, TargetSelector — all async: true
- Target-not-found renders the correct warning without crashing
- Cancel from inventory or target selector returns {:continue, state} with no message

title: Implement buildings (enter/exit directions, interior blocks)
Simplify Player facade with Player.Server.call/3
description:
Every public function in Player (apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/player.ex) follows the same two-line pattern:

    start_if_inactive(player_id)
    GenServer.call(PlayerServer.via(player_id), message)

This is repeated across ~10 functions. The proposal is to add a Player.Server.call/3 that centralises both the lazy-start and the GenServer dispatch:

    def call(player_id, supervisor, message) do
      ensure_started(player_id, supervisor)
      GenServer.call(via(player_id), message)
    end

The supervisor is passed as an argument (rather than aliased directly) to avoid a circular dependency — Player.Server would otherwise need to reference PlayerSupervisor, which already supervises Player.Server. Player can pass PlayerSupervisor in at the call site.

With this in place:
- Player facade functions reduce to a single line: PlayerServer.call(player_id, PlayerSupervisor, message)
- start_if_inactive/1 moves into Player.Server (as a private ensure_started/2)
- via/1 and process_name/1 can become private on Player.Server
- Player.Server still needs to expose start_link/1 and nothing else publicly

Note: perform/3 in Player has slightly different logic (pattern-matches on {:ok, ap} vs {:error, _} after the GenServer call) so may need a small wrapper rather than a plain call/3 delegation.

Key files:
- apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/player.ex — facade to simplify
- apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/player/server.ex — add call/3, make via/process_name private
- apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/player/supervisor.ex — check what it currently exposes (start_player/2)
- apps/undercity_server/test/undercity_server/player_test.exs — should need no changes if public API is unchanged

Refactor so the GenServer only returns {id, people} and the Gateway (client-side interface) assembles the full block_info by calling WorldMap functions itself. This keeps the GenServer simple ('what's happening in this block right now') and moves world-structure queries to the caller's node. This change should be transparent to the CLI.
notes: Keys to move out of GenServer: :type (from WorldMap @block_types), :neighbourhood (WorldMap.block_context/1), :buildings (WorldMap.building_names/0), :building_type (WorldMap.building_type/1). Only :id and :people are truly dynamic. Parent bead: 5zq.

title: Design inventory architecture
description: Settle the architecture decisions around player inventory before implementing the Search feature (1gr). Decide where inventory lives (expand Person struct vs separate player state process/store), how it's persisted, and how it interacts with blocks. Deliverable is a design plan documented in the bead's design field.
design:
## Decision: Player GenServer per player

Player state will live in a dedicated GenServer per player, not embedded in Block state.

### Reasoning
- Blocks currently store full Person structs in their people MapSet (DETS-persisted). This doesn't scale as player state grows (inventory, AP, health).
- Inventory alone could work with a simple ETS/DETS table, but future state (health modified by other players/server, AP with timers) needs mailbox serialization and process behavior.
- Player GenServer is the right home for all mutable player state.

### Persistence
- Single shared DETS table (players.dets) rather than file-per-player.
- Blocks are fixed/known entities so file-per-block makes sense. Players are dynamic — single table is simpler to manage and enables cross-player queries.
- One PlayerStore process manages the table; Player GenServers read/write through it.

### Lifecycle
- Start with always-on (like blocks) for simplicity. Optimize to on-demand with timeout later if player count demands it.

### Scaling note
- Wrap player process lookup in a function (e.g. Player.via(id)) so all callsites use indirection. Today returns {name, node}, later can be swapped for distributed registry (Horde) or consistent hash ring with no GenServer code changes.
- Single DETS table may eventually move to ETS + snapshots or SQLite at ~10K+ players.

## Decision: Person struct removed, Player state in server layer

### Core domain (undercity_core)
- No player identity or IDs — pure game logic only.
- Modules like Inventory, future Health, ActionPoints operate on data structs.
- Functions take data in, return data out. No OTP dependencies.

### Server layer (undercity_server)
- Player GenServer holds {id, name, inventory, ...} and calls core functions to mutate state.
- PlayerIdentity module owns all identity concerns: ID generation, via(id) process lookup, name-to-ID resolution, process name formatting.
- PlayerStore manages persistence (single DETS table).
- Person module is removed. Blocks store a MapSet of player IDs only.

### Hydration pattern
- Blocks are self-contained for 'who is present' (set of player IDs).
- Gateway hydrates display data (names, etc.) via a batch PlayerStore.get_names(ids) call — single hop, not per-player messaging.
- Caching layer (ETS read-through or Gateway-level) can be added in front of PlayerStore later since names rarely change.

## Decision: Player processes run server-side

- Player GenServers live on the server node alongside Block GenServers.
- All game logic (search, combat, etc.) is authoritative server-side.
- Block-to-Player coordination is local calls (no network hop).
- PlayerSupervisor, PlayerStore, and Player GenServers live under the server application's supervision tree.
- Actions like searching naturally read from both Block state (what can be found) and Player state (inventory capacity). Block state will directly influence mechanics.
notes:
## NPC consideration (far future, not relevant to search/inventory work)
Zombies would use a 'blob' model — one GenServer per block holding aggregate zombie state, driven by a periodic behavior tree tick. Different OTP structure from players (N:1 vs 1:1) but shares core domain modules (Health, Combat). Fits naturally in OTP — GenServer with Process.send_after for ticks, supervised per-block, message passing between neighbouring blobs for splitting/merging. No impact on current design decisions.

title: Server: Player.receive_heal/2 + Actions.UseItem
description:
Add Player.receive_heal/2 (inverse of take_damage) to the server Player module and Player.Server GenServer, then implement Actions.UseItem following the attack action pattern. Wire into Gateway.

## New files
- apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/actions/use_item.ex

## Changes
- apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/player.ex — add receive_heal/2 public function
- apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/player/server.ex — add handle_call({:receive_heal, amount})
- apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/gateway.ex — add dispatch clause for :use_item

## Player.receive_heal/2
Public: Player.receive_heal(player_id, amount)
Calls handle_call({:receive_heal, amount}).
GenServer callback applies Health.apply_effect(health, {:heal, amount}), persists via save!, returns {:ok, new_hp}.
Mirrors take_damage exactly.

## Actions.UseItem
Follows the attack action pattern (with pipeline):
  1. Block.has_person?(block_id, target_id) — validate target is in same block
     (For self-targeting, target_id == player_id; no self-exclusion guard needed — this is intentional, unlike attack)
  2. Look up item name from inventory index (or accept item name directly — TBD during implementation)
  3. Player.use_item(user_id, item_name, cost) — atomically consumes item + spends AP
  4. On success: Player.receive_heal(target_id, 5)
  5. Return {:ok, {:healed, target_id, 5}, remaining_ap}

## Gateway
New dispatch clause:
  defp dispatch(player_id, block_id, :use_item, {target_id, item_index}),
    do: Actions.UseItem.use_item(player_id, block_id, target_id, item_index)

## Acceptance criteria
- Heals self: HP increases by 5 (capped at 50), item removed, AP spent
- Heals target in same block: target HP increases, caster item removed, caster AP spent
- Target at full HP (50): item still consumed, AP still spent, HP stays at 50
- Target not in same block: {:error, :invalid_target}, item untouched, AP unchanged
- Item not in inventory: {:error, :item_missing}, AP unchanged
- AP exhausted: {:error, :exhausted}, item untouched
- Collapsed caster: {:error, :collapsed}, item untouched
- DESIGN DECISION NEEDED: collapsed target (0 HP) — can they be healed? Default assumption: yes (receive_heal does not check collapse). Confirm with user before implementing.

## Test file
apps/undercity_server/test/undercity_server/actions/use_item_test.exs (new)
Model after search_test.exs: async: true, uses Helpers.start_player!/1 and start_block!, asserts on return value and resulting player state.
notes: Design decisions locked: (1) Collapsed target cannot be healed — add check in Actions.UseItem before calling receive_heal. If target HP is 0, return {:error, :target_collapsed} and leave item and AP untouched. (2) Unified :use_item action for both self and other-targeting.

title: Fix CRLF line endings across project
description:
mix format --check-formatted fails on many files due to CRLF (Windows) line endings. The formatter expects LF. Affected files include mix.exs, config/config.exs, app mix.exs files, test files, and module files that haven't been reformatted yet. This causes mix lint to fail before credo even runs.

Root cause: git is checking out files with CRLF on Windows (git config core.autocrlf is likely true or not set). The Elixir formatter normalizes to LF, so any file that hasn't been through mix format will fail.

Fix options:
1. Add a .gitattributes file with '* text=auto eol=lf' to force LF in the repo
2. Run 'mix format' across all files to normalize, then commit
3. Both — .gitattributes prevents recurrence, format fixes existing files

Files currently failing:
- mix.exs
- config/config.exs
- apps/undercity_core/mix.exs
- apps/undercity_core/test/undercity_core_test.exs
- apps/undercity_core/lib/undercity_core.ex (was fixed this session but will recur on fresh checkout)
- apps/undercity_cli/mix.exs
- apps/undercity_cli/test/undercity_cli_test.exs
- apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli.ex

Note: During this session, every time we edited a file we had to run mix format after to fix the CRLF issue introduced by git checkout restoring CRLF endings.
notes: Previous attempt in bead ncy (Configure Credo) added .gitattributes with eol=lf and normalized line endings. This was merged in PR #3 (commit 6a6803d 'add mix lint alias and normalize line endings'). However the fix didn't fully stick — files are still being checked out with CRLF on Windows. The .gitattributes may not be configured correctly, or git's core.autocrlf setting on this machine is overriding it. Need to investigate why the previous .gitattributes fix from ncy isn't working.

title: Refactor CLI view into OWL components
description:
Refactor the CLI view into components using Owl LiveScreen. Register distinct UI blocks (vicinity, message) with LiveScreen and update state instead of clearing and reprinting the whole screen on every action.

Plan:
1. Extract View.Grid — grid rendering (render_grid, render_row, render_cell_lines, pad_center)
2. Extract View.Description — block description text (describe_block, describe_people, scribble)
3. Extract View.Status — tier logic and status/threshold messages, format_message
4. Create View.Screen — LiveScreen integration, registers blocks and handles updates
5. Slim GameLoop — input parsing and dispatch only, calls View.Screen for display
6. Wire up LiveScreen — replace clear-and-rewrite with LiveScreen block updates
design:
## LiveScreen Integration (Surroundings only)

### How Owl.LiveScreen works
- Auto-started by :owl application as a named GenServer (Owl.LiveScreen)
- Since :owl is a dep of undercity_cli, LiveScreen is already running
- Blocks are sticky regions at the bottom of terminal, re-rendered in-place
- add_block(id, state: initial, render: fn state -> Owl.Data.t end)
- update(id, new_state) triggers re-render on next tick (default 60ms)
- IO.puts to the LiveScreen device prints above sticky blocks
- IO.gets returns {:error, :enotsup} — input must use Owl.IO or read from :stdio directly

### Owl.IO for input
- Owl.IO.input/1 reads from :stdio directly (not through LiveScreen), works alongside live blocks
- Renders a blue "> " prompt, supports casting/validation/re-prompting
- Could replace our IO.gets("> ") for a more polished prompt, but not required for initial integration
- Other Owl.IO functions: confirm, select, multiselect — useful for future menus

### Plan for surroundings block
1. Split Surroundings.render into pure function (returns grid string) and IO wrapper
2. Register :surroundings block at game start with render callback using the pure function
3. Call Owl.LiveScreen.update(:surroundings, vicinity) instead of Surroundings.render
4. Block description, status messages, constitution stay as direct IO.puts for now
5. Input stays on :stdio (IO.gets or Owl.IO.input) — no conflict with LiveScreen

### Open question
- Do we use LiveScreen as the IO device for non-block output (messages appear above blocks)?
- Or keep non-block output on :stdio and only use LiveScreen for the surroundings block?
- Answer: start minimal — just surroundings as a block, everything else stays as-is
notes:
## Progress

### Completed
- Steps 1-3: Extracted View submodules (Surroundings, BlockDescription, Constitution, Status)
- Pushed IO into View layer — submodules handle their own rendering
- Moved render entry point from GameLoop into View
- Extracted action rendering from GameLoop handle_ functions into View (render_move, render_search, render_inventory, render_drop, render_eat, render_scribble, render_unknown_command)
- GameLoop now: call Gateway, call View, return state

### Next step
- Introduce Owl.LiveScreen (https://hexdocs.pm/owl/Owl.LiveScreen.html) for rendering surroundings only
- Other parts of the view (block description, constitution, status messages) stay as direct IO for now
- Incrementally migrate remaining view components to LiveScreen blocks after surroundings is proven out

title: Add /architect command for linear growth detection
description: A slash command (/architect) that uses git history to identify files and modules showing linear growth patterns — files that consistently receive additive changes over time without corresponding reorganisation. The agent flags these areas as potential future maintainability problems (e.g. a GenServer that keeps getting new callbacks, a module that grows by one function per feature). Output is a short, ranked list of concerns. The user can then choose to discuss any flagged area further for deeper investigation and brainstorming.
acceptance_criteria:
- A .claude/commands/architect.md file exists defining the /architect slash command
- The command gathers multiple growth signals: git log --numstat (net line delta, commit frequency, add:delete ratio), current file size, public function count (def count), and last-touched recency
- Add:delete ratio is treated as a first-class signal — a high ratio (e.g. 10:1) indicates accumulation rather than evolution
- Recent growth rate is compared against lifetime rate to detect acceleration; files speeding up in recent commits are prioritised over files that simply got large historically
- Public function count (grep for 'def ') is used as the structural signal in Elixir — a GenServer accumulating exported functions is the real symptom, not just line count
- Long-dormant files (not touched recently) are deprioritised or excluded — focus is on files that will continue to grow
- The agent identifies a shortlist of files exhibiting growth symptoms — it does NOT diagnose root causes or suggest solutions
- Output per flagged file: filename, growth pattern summary, and relevant indicators (e.g. add:delete ratio, touched in X of last N commits, net +Y lines, N exported functions)
- The agent selects a small number of concerns to present and invites the user to choose which to explore in more detail
- No solutions, no recommendations — diagnosis only at this stage
notes:
Triggered by observing UndercityServer.Player constantly requiring new methods per feature — a pattern of linear accumulation rather than evolution.

Scope: analysis is limited to apps/*/lib/** — production source code only. Test files, config, mix files, and docs are excluded; growth in those areas is expected and not a signal.

Growth signals to gather:
- git log --numstat: net line delta per file, commit frequency, add:delete ratio (high ratio = accumulation, not evolution; a file with 10:1 add:delete is piling up, not being reworked)
- Recency weighting: compare recent growth rate (last N commits) against lifetime rate to detect acceleration — a file speeding up is more concerning than one that grew slowly over years
- Public function count: grep for 'def ' growth over time; in Elixir, accumulating exported functions is the structural symptom, not just raw line count
- Current file size (wc -l): used in combination with other signals, not in isolation
- Last-touched recency: deprioritise or exclude long-dormant files; the concern is active growth that will continue, not historical size

The agent shortlists the most notable cases (not exhaustive) and lets the user steer the deeper conversation. Long-dormant large files should be filtered out — only actively-growing files are relevant.

title: Add UndercityCore.Player struct to centralise player business logic
description:
## Problem

Player business logic is currently split between UndercityCore (AP, Health, Inventory as isolated modules) and the UndercityServer.Player GenServer (which assembles and mutates them). The GenServer's handle_call implementations contain the actual game rules — eating costs AP, you can't act when collapsed, eating removes the item and applies the health effect — as procedural steps inside OTP callbacks.

This makes the rules hard to test (requires a live GenServer), hard to find (buried in callbacks), and easy to get wrong if a new action forgets a step.

## Proposed Solution

Add `%UndercityCore.Player{}` as a pure struct with pure action functions. The struct owns the intrinsic player state:

```elixir
defmodule UndercityCore.Player do
  defstruct [:id, :name, :inventory, :action_points, :health]
end
```

Note: no `block_id` field. Block membership stays implicit in Block GenServers (avoids dual-source-of-truth for location).

## How This Reduces Server Complexity

The key win is that GenServer handle_calls become thin load→delegate→save wrappers, and the rules move into testable pure functions in Core.

### Example: Eat

**Current — rules live inside OTP callback:**
```elixir
def handle_call({:eat_item, index}, _from, state) do
  items = Inventory.list_items(state.inventory)
  with {:index, true} <- {:index, index >= 0 and index < length(items)},
       item = Enum.at(items, index),
       {:edible, effect} when effect != :not_edible <- {:edible, Food.effect(item.name)},
       {:ok, state} <- exert(state, 1) do
    health = Health.apply_effect(state.health, effect)
    inventory = Inventory.remove_at(state.inventory, index)
    state = %{state | inventory: inventory, health: health}
    PlayerStore.save(state.id, state)
    {:reply, {:ok, item, effect, ActionPoints.current(state.action_points), Health.current(health)}, state}
  else
    ...
  end
end
```

**With Core.Player — rules live in Core, GenServer just persists:**
```elixir
# Core (pure, testable):
def eat(%Player{} = player, index, now) do
  with {:ok, item, inventory} <- take_item(player.inventory, index),
       {:edible, effect} when effect != :not_edible <- {:edible, Food.effect(item.name)},
       {:ok, player} <- exert(player, 1, now) do
    {:ok, %{player | inventory: inventory, health: Health.apply_effect(player.health, effect)}, item, effect}
  end
end

# GenServer handle_call (thin wrapper):
def handle_call({:eat_item, index}, _from, state) do
  case Player.eat(state, index, now()) do
    {:ok, state, item, effect} ->
      PlayerStore.save(state.id, state)
      {:reply, {:ok, item, effect, ActionPoints.current(state.action_points), Health.current(state.health)}, state}
    {:error, _} = error ->
      {:reply, error, state}
  end
end
```

### Example: Drop item

The `exert/2` private function (collapsed check + AP regen + AP spend) currently only exists inside the GenServer and is duplicated nowhere but also untestable. Moving it to `Core.Player.exert/3` means it becomes a single tested function that all actions share.

### Example: Search

Search currently splits across two GenServer calls (Block.search → Player.add_item) coordinated in Actions.Search. With a Core Player struct the result is the same OTP-wise, but `Player.receive_item/2` becomes a named, tested pure function rather than an inline Inventory mutation.

## What Stays in the Server

- GenServer lifecycle (start/stop, process naming, supervision)
- Persistence (PlayerStore.save calls)
- Anything requiring two processes (movement block coordination)

## Out of Scope

- block_id on the player struct (kept implicit in Block membership)
- Movement (stays as server-side OTP coordination)
design:
## Implementation Plan

### 1. Create UndercityCore.Player
File: apps/undercity_core/lib/undercity_core/player.ex (new)

Struct:
  defstruct [:id, :name, :inventory, :action_points, :health]
  # No block_id — location stays implicit in Block GenServers

Pure functions:
- new/2            — replaces manual state construction in GenServer init/1
- exert/2          — collapsed check + AP regen + AP spend (currently untestable private GenServer fn)
- drop/2           — index check + exert + inventory removal, returns {:ok, player, item_name}
- eat/2            — index check + food effect check + exert + apply effect + inventory removal

consume_item is inventory-level logic, not player-level — leave it as a private function
in the GenServer or consider moving to the Inventory module separately.

### 2. Refactor UndercityServer.Player GenServer
File: apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/player.ex

- init/1: call Player.new(id, name) instead of constructing a plain map
- All handle_call clauses become thin load → Player.<action>(state, args) → save! → reply
- Remove private exert/2 (moves to Core)
- GenServer state becomes %UndercityCore.Player{} struct

### 3. DETS migration
No migration needed — reset existing data with mix reset before deploying.
We have no production data worth preserving at this stage.

### 4. Tests
File: apps/undercity_core/test/undercity_core/player_test.exs (new)

Add unit tests covering all new Core.Player functions:
- new/2 — correct initial state
- exert/2 — success, :exhausted, :collapsed
- drop/2 — success, :invalid_index, :exhausted, :collapsed
- eat/2  — success, :invalid_index, :not_edible, :exhausted, :collapsed

These run without a GenServer — fast, async: true, no setup overhead.

### Files
New:  apps/undercity_core/lib/undercity_core/player.ex
New:  apps/undercity_core/test/undercity_core/player_test.exs
Mod:  apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/player.ex

title: Choose language and tech stack
description: Decide on the programming language and key libraries for the CLI version. Consider: language suitability for later porting to browser (e.g. TypeScript/Node works for both CLI and web), ease of text-based UI, state management approach, and data persistence story.
notes:
## Tech Stack Decision

- **Language**: Elixir
- **Core**: Elixir/OTP — game logic, state, world simulation
- **Client (initial)**: CLI client in Elixir
- **Persistence**: None initially, add later
- **Web client**: Phoenix later, not in initial scope
- **Architecture**: Core separated from client frontends — core is the authority, clients are thin

### Rationale
OTP supervision trees map naturally to game world entities (tiles, zombies, weather). Actor model suits concurrent independent processes. Fault tolerance built in. Clean path from CLI to web client without changing the core.

### Deferred decisions (to explore in technical design)
- Process architecture (tile GenServers, player processes, etc.)
- AP recharge mechanism
- Client-core interface (same node vs network protocol)
- Persistence layer
