title: Fix flaky player eat_item test
description:
## Failure

The test 'eat_item/2 applies heal effect to health' in apps/undercity_server/test/undercity_server/player_test.exs:139 fails intermittently with:

  RuntimeError: failed to start child with the spec {UndercityServer.Player, [id: "player_eat_39705", name: "test_player_eat_39705"]}.
  Reason: bad child specification, got: {:already_started, #PID<...>}

## Cause

The test runs 100 iterations, each spawning a Player process with a random ID using :rand.uniform(100_000). When two iterations land on the same random number, start_supervised!/2 tries to start a process with an already-registered ID, causing the crash.

## Discovery

Surfaced while running the full test suite during unrelated CLI refactoring (bead 2se). The failure is non-deterministic and seed-dependent — it passed on most runs but failed on some.

## Fix

Replace the random ID generation with something guaranteed unique per iteration, e.g. using a counter, make_ref/0, or :erlang.unique_integer/1.
close_reason: Fixed in branch c0i-fix-flaky-player-eat-item-test. Replaced :rand.uniform with :erlang.unique_integer([:positive]) across all affected async test modules.

title: CLI game: add visual grid of current block and neighbours
description:
Add a visual map grid to the CLI game view showing the player's current block and its neighbours. The grid should resemble the 3x3 world layout from 4ah.2:

  Ashwell       | North Alley   | Wormgarden
  West Street   | [The Plaza]   | East Street
  The Stray     | South Alley   | Lame Horse Inn

Highlight the player's current block. Below the grid, show the description of the current block (and possibly neighbours). The grid data is available from WorldMap and Block exits. The View module (undercity_cli/view.ex) is where rendering lives.
notes:
## Visual Design

The grid is always 3x3 with the player's current block in the center cell. Neighbours fill adjacent cells based on exits. Empty cells appear at map edges where no block exists.

### Grid Rendering

```
┌─────────────────┬─────────────────┬─────────────────┐
│    Ashwell      │  North Alley    │   Wormgarden    │
├─────────────────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────┤
│  West Street    │ ** The Plaza ** │  East Street    │
├─────────────────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────┤
│   The Stray     │  South Alley    │ Lame Horse Inn  │
└─────────────────┴─────────────────┴─────────────────┘

The Plaza
The central gathering place of the undercity.

Exits: north, south, east, west
Also here: Garrett

> _
```

At a corner (e.g. Ashwell, NW):

```
┌─────────────────┬─────────────────┬─────────────────┐
│                 │                 │                 │
├─────────────────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────┤
│                 │ ** Ashwell **   │  North Alley    │
├─────────────────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────┤
│                 │  West Street    │  The Plaza      │
└─────────────────┴─────────────────┴─────────────────┘
```

At an edge (e.g. East Street):

```
┌─────────────────┬─────────────────┬─────────────────┐
│  North Alley    │   Wormgarden    │                 │
├─────────────────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────┤
│  The Plaza      │** East Street **│                 │
├─────────────────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────┤
│  South Alley    │ Lame Horse Inn  │                 │
└─────────────────┴─────────────────┴─────────────────┘
```

### Color

- Center cell (player location): use the existing block name color (\e[38;5;103m, purplish)
- Neighbour cells: grey (\e[38;5;245m, same as description text)
- Grid borders: default terminal color
- Below the grid, description/exits/people use existing View colors

### Implementation

- Grid is rendered in View module (undercity_cli/view.ex)
- Player's current block is always center cell
- Neighbours are determined from WorldMap adjacency (not just exits — need diagonals too: NW, NE, SW, SE)
- WorldMap already defines the full 3x3 layout; can derive diagonal neighbours from grid coordinates
- Cell width should be fixed (pad/truncate block names to fit)
- The whole screen clears and redraws each frame (see undercity-lbj)

title: Dynamic space descriptions based on building type
description: Space blocks (exterior areas in front of buildings) currently use a single static description. Instead, the description should vary based on the linked building's interior type — e.g. a space outside an inn should read differently than one outside a blacksmith.
notes:
## Design context

Space blocks have type :space and connect to a building interior via an :enter exit. The interior block has a type like :inn or :blacksmith.

To make descriptions dynamic, the view layer needs to know the interior block's type. Options:
- Look up the :enter exit destination in WorldMap and resolve its type at render time
- Pass the building type as extra data in block_info from the server

The description map in View would change from a single :space key to per-interior-type space descriptions, e.g.:
- space_inn: "A crooked timber frame leans over the street, firelight bleeding through shuttered windows."
- space_blacksmith: "Heat radiates from a squat stone building. The clang of iron carries through thin walls."

Refer to STYLE_GUIDE.md for tone.

title: Scribbling
description: Allow players to use Chalk to scribble messages on blocks. Scribbles are anonymous, persisted, and shown as part of the block description.
notes:
## Design

### Rules
- Chalk can be used to scribble a message on the current block
- Scribbles are persisted on the block — one scribble per block, new scribbles overwrite the previous
- Chalk has 5 uses, then it is consumed and removed from inventory
- Scribbles shown in block description: "Someone has scribbled <message in italics>"
- Author is anonymous
- Works on any block (inside or outside buildings — these are separate blocks so no special handling needed)

### Constraints
- Max 80 characters per scribble
- Alphanumeric characters only (plus spaces)

### Notes
- Scribble data lives on the Block struct/DETS since it's block state, not player state
- Chalk uses could be tracked as item durability (integer field on the item)

title: Buildings
description: Add buildings to blocks, Urban Dead style. Each block may contain 0-1 buildings based on its block type. Buildings are self-contained spaces that players can enter and exit.
notes:
## Design

### Core rules
- A block may have 0 or 1 buildings, determined by its block type
- Buildings have their own name, description, and player list
- Buildings can only be entered from the block they are located on
- Exiting a building returns the player to the parent block
- Players inside a building cannot see block details (players, description, etc.) and vice versa — buildings and blocks are fully separate contexts

### Architecture decision: buildings are blocks
A building is just a block that isn't on the grid. It reuses the exact same OTP infrastructure — BlockSupervisor, Block GenServer, Store, DETS persistence. No new process types needed.

Buildings connect to their parent block via exits. Movement in/out uses the same Gateway.move/3 flow as cardinal directions. The direction type expands from :north/:south/:east/:west to include :enter/:exit (shorthand: in/out).

Example for The Lame Horse Inn:
- Grid block `lame_horse` (type: :space) has exits: %{north: "east_street", west: "south_alley", enter: "lame_horse_interior"}
- Interior block `lame_horse_interior` (type: :inn) has exits: %{exit: "lame_horse"}

### Block type changes
With buildings, the block type system splits into three categories:

**Exterior blocks without buildings** (on grid):
- :street, :square, :graveyard, :fountain
- CLI: "You are at <Name>. <type description>"

**Exterior blocks with buildings** (on grid):
- :space — a new type for the outdoor area in front of a building
- CLI: "You are outside <Name>."
- Description should vary based on the building's interior type (e.g. a space outside an inn describes differently than one outside a blacksmith). Implementation TBD.

**Building interiors** (not on grid):
- :inn, :blacksmith, (future interior types)
- CLI: "You are inside <Name>. <type description>"

### What changes
- Block direction type: add :enter and :exit
- WorldMap: define buildings alongside blocks, wire enter/exit connections
- View: handle at/outside/inside prefix based on block category
- View: space descriptions derived from the linked building's interior type (design TBD)
- Neighbourhood grid: probably hidden when inside a building

### What doesn't change
- Block GenServer, Store, BlockSupervisor — completely untouched
- join/leave/find_person API — identical
- DETS persistence — same structure
- Gateway movement logic — same move/3 flow

### Future: multi-block buildings (out of scope)
Large buildings that span multiple blocks (e.g. a 2x4 cathedral) will come later. For now, buildings are strictly 1:1 with their parent block.
close_reason: Buildings implemented with grid rendering and dynamic descriptions

title: Resolve pre-existing UndercityCore references in CLI
description:
## Background

The architecture doc states (docs/architecture.md:23):

> **CLI knows nothing about core**: Despite the umbrella dependency allowing it, \`undercity_cli\` intentionally does not reference \`undercity_core\` types directly. All interaction goes through \`Gateway\` and the types it exposes (e.g. \`Vicinity\`). This keeps the client decoupled from domain internals.

Two pre-existing violations of this rule exist in the CLI. Decide how to resolve each — either fix the violation, or update the architecture doc to reflect an accepted exception.

## Violations

### 1. `apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/view/inventory_selector.ex:16`

```elixir
@spec select([UndercityCore.Item.t()], Owl.Data.t()) :: {:ok, non_neg_integer()} | :cancel
```

A typespec references `UndercityCore.Item.t()` directly. No functional calls to core — purely a type annotation. Options: replace with a local type alias, remove the spec, expose `Item.t()` via the server layer, or accept as a documented exception.

### 2. `apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli.ex:25` (moduledoc only)

```
The CLI intentionally does not reference `UndercityCore`
```

This is just the moduledoc restating the architecture rule — not a code violation. No action needed here.

## Questions to resolve

- Should `UndercityCore.Item.t()` be re-exported or aliased through the server layer so CLI can reference it without a direct core dep?
- Or is a typespec-only reference an acceptable exception to document?
- Should `undercity_core` be added to `undercity_cli`'s mix.exs deps for type-checking purposes, or is the current implicit dep (via server) sufficient?

title: Reconnecting - existing players resume instead of respawning
Flash message buffer for View
description: Replace direct View.render_messages calls with a flash-style message buffer that accumulates messages and flushes them once per input loop. Currently LiveScreen :messages block replaces state on each update, so rapid updates overwrite previous messages. Commands currently build and render messages inline, including threshold messages. Future pub/sub damage messages will arrive asynchronously and need to accumulate without overwriting unread messages. Design: Add a message buffer that accumulates messages. Commands push via View.flash instead of View.render_messages. Game loop flushes after each dispatch. Threshold messages can be handled automatically in the loop by diffing ap/hp before/after dispatch, removing that logic from every command. Buffer storage likely needs Agent for cross-process pub/sub access. Move surroundings/description updates stay as direct calls.

title: Expand UndercityCore.Search to own the full block search pipeline
description:
## Problem

UndercityCore.Search.search/2 is a single-line wrapper over LootTable.roll/2 that adds no value. The actual search pipeline (get loot table for block type, then roll it) is split across two calls at the call site.

## Solution

Change the function signature to take block_type instead of a pre-built loot_table, and have Search own the full pipeline:

```elixir
# Before (current — caller must know about LootTable internals):
loot_table = LootTable.for_block_type(block_type)
Search.search(loot_table, roll)

# After (caller just knows the block type):
Search.search(block_type, roll \ :rand.uniform())
# Internally: block_type |> LootTable.for_block_type() |> LootTable.roll(roll)
```

## Benefits

- Search becomes a meaningful abstraction with a clear purpose
- Single place to add future complexity (search modifiers, AP-scaled probabilities, etc.)
- Callers no longer need to import or know about LootTable
- The intent at the call site matches what callers actually know (block type, not loot table)

title: Core: healing item domain model and loot table entry
description:
Add the domain-layer foundation for healing items: an item registry module, a heal action on the Player struct, and a loot table entry so the item appears in the world.

## What to build

1. A new module (e.g. UndercityCore.Healer, mirroring UndercityCore.Food) that:
   - Maps item names to a fixed heal amount, e.g. %{"Bandage" => {:heal, 10}}
   - Exposes healable?(item_name) :: boolean and effect(item_name) :: {:heal, pos_integer()} | :not_healable
   - Heal amount is deterministic (not random), unlike Food which picks randomly

2. UndercityCore.Player.heal/3 — a new function parallel to Player.eat/3 that:
   - Validates the index is in bounds (returns {:error, :invalid_index} otherwise)
   - Validates the item is healable via Healer.effect/1 (returns {:error, :not_healable, item_name} otherwise)
   - Calls exert(player, 1, now) for AP gating (returns {:error, :exhausted} or {:error, :collapsed})
   - Removes the item from inventory
   - Applies the heal effect via Health.apply_effect/2
   - Returns {:ok, player, item, {:heal, amount}}

3. LootTable: add the healing item to at least one block type table. The inn block type (currently only Iron Pipe at 0.40) is the natural fit. Probability TBD by content decisions but 0.30 is a reasonable starting point.

## Item naming

Item name must be chosen to fit the dark fantasy setting — consult STYLE_GUIDE.md. Working name is "Bandage" but should be reviewed. The name is referenced in Healer's @table, the loot table, and CLI rendering.

## Constraints

- Healer module must be pure (no OTP, no side effects) — same rule as Food.
- Item.new/1 is used (no uses field needed — the item is fully consumed on use, so remove_at is the right pattern, not Item.use/1).
- Do not modify Health or Inventory — heal applies via the existing Health.apply_effect/2.
- HP is clamped to max by Health.apply_effect — no special handling needed.

## Acceptance criteria

- healable?("Bandage") returns true; healable?("Mushroom") returns false
- effect("Bandage") returns {:heal, N} for a fixed positive N
- Player.heal/3 on a valid healable item returns {:ok, updated_player, item, {:heal, N}}
- Player.heal/3 spends 1 AP
- Player.heal/3 removes the item from inventory
- Player.heal/3 returns {:error, :not_healable, item_name} for a non-healable item without spending AP
- Player.heal/3 returns {:error, :invalid_index} for out-of-bounds index
- Player.heal/3 returns {:error, :exhausted} when player has no AP
- Player.heal/3 returns {:error, :collapsed} when player HP is 0
- HP after healing is clamped to Health.max() — healing a full-health player does not overflow
- The healing item appears in the loot table for at least one block type

## Test cases (core, async: true)

- UndercityCore.HealerTest: healable?/1 and effect/1 cases
- UndercityCore.PlayerTest in describe "heal/3": mirrors the eat/3 test structure, covering success, AP spend, item removal, not_healable, invalid_index, exhausted, collapsed
notes: Experts consulted: undercity-core-expert, test-expert, content-writer. This bead has no upstream dependencies — implement first.

title: Server: heal action pipeline for self and others
description:
Add the server-layer action for healing. Supports two cases: healing self (item from own inventory, apply HP to self) and healing a target in the same block (item from healer's inventory, apply HP to target).

## What to build

### Player GenServer additions (Player.Server + Player facade)

1. Player.heal_self/1 — GenServer callback {:heal_self} that:
   - Calls Player.heal/3 (the core function from the core sub-task) on the player struct
   - On success, saves state and replies {:ok, item, {:heal, amount}, new_ap, new_hp}
   - On error, replies the error tuple unchanged

2. Player.apply_heal/2 — GenServer callback {:apply_heal, amount} that:
   - Applies Health.apply_effect(health, {:heal, amount}) to the target player
   - Saves state
   - Returns {:ok, new_hp}
   - This is the target-side call, analogous to take_damage

3. Expose both via the Player facade module (mirroring how drop_item, eat_item, take_damage are exposed).

### Actions.Heal module

Implements Actions.Heal.heal(player_id, block_id, target_id, item_index):
- If target_id == player_id: heal self (call Player.heal_self, no block check needed beyond eat's approach)
- If target_id != player_id: validate target is in block (Block.has_person?), then use Player.perform/3 to spend AP, consume item from healer's inventory, and call Player.apply_heal on target
- Returns result shapes consistent with Gateway conventions

Result shapes:
  - Self-heal success: {:ok, item, {:heal, amount}, new_ap, new_hp}
  - Other-heal success: {:ok, item, {:heal, amount}, target_id, new_ap, healer_hp}
    (healer's HP is unchanged on other-heal but returned for consistent CLI state update)
  - Errors: {:error, :not_healable, item_name}, {:error, :invalid_index}, {:error, :exhausted}, {:error, :collapsed}, {:error, :invalid_target}

### Gateway addition

Add a perform clause:
  def perform(player_id, block_id, :heal, {target_id, item_index}) -> Actions.Heal.heal(...)

Since eat bypasses block validation (it is not dispatched through the block-check path), heal targeting self should follow the same pattern. Healing others must go through the block-check dispatch path.

## Constraints

- Other-heal must validate that target is in the same block as the healer.
- Cannot heal a collapsed player (HP already 0) — the action may still succeed (it applies HP) but note that collapsed means HP == 0. Applying heal to a collapsed player should restore HP and allow them to act again. This is intentional: healing is the recovery mechanism.
- The healer's item is always consumed from the healer's inventory (not the target's).
- AP is always spent from the healer, not the target.
- Player.Server must persist state after every mutation (both healer and target saves).

## Acceptance criteria

- Healing self: item consumed, HP increased (clamped to max), AP spent, persisted
- Healing another player in the same block: item consumed from healer, target HP increased, healer AP spent, both saves persisted
- Healing a player not in the block returns {:error, :invalid_target}
- Healing self with a non-healable item returns {:error, :not_healable, item_name}
- Healing with no AP returns {:error, :exhausted}
- Healing while collapsed returns {:error, :collapsed}
- Gateway routes :heal correctly

## Test cases (server, async: true using Test.Helpers)

- Player.Server tests for :heal_self and :apply_heal callbacks (start_player!, assert state changes)
- Actions.Heal tests: success self, success other, invalid_target (target not in block), not_healable, exhausted, collapsed
notes: Experts consulted: undercity-server-expert, test-expert. Depends on: undercity-go7.1 (core domain model must exist first).

title: CLI: heal command with target selector
description:
Add the CLI command for healing. Mirrors the attack command's selector pipeline: bare 'heal' opens a target selector (including self), and 'heal <target>' or 'heal <target> <n>' allow direct dispatch.

## What to build

### Commands.Heal module

Implements dispatch/4 (and dispatch/5 with injected selectors for testability).

Command syntax (reflected in usage/0):
  heal [target] [n]

Dispatch pipeline:
- 'heal' (bare) — open target selector showing all people in vicinity plus self, then open inventory selector → dispatch
- 'heal <target>' — skip target selector, open inventory selector → dispatch
- 'heal <target> <n>' — skip both selectors, dispatch directly with 1-based item index

Target selector for heal differs from attack: it must include the player themselves as a valid target (self-heal). The TargetSelector currently operates on vicinity.people which excludes self. A 'heal' command should prepend a 'Yourself' or the player's own name option. Consider passing a synthetic people list with the player prepended, then mapping the selection back to a target_id (if self is selected, use state.player_id as target_id).

### Rendering

On success — self-heal:
  "You use a <item_name> and restore <amount> HP."

On success — healing another:
  "You use a <item_name> on <target_name> and restore <amount> HP."

On error — not_healable:
  "You can't use <item_name> as a healing item."

On error — invalid_target:
  "<target_name> is not here."

On error — invalid_index:
  "Invalid item selection."

Exhausted and collapsed are handled centrally by Commands.handle_action/4 (no special casing needed).

### Registration

Add {Commands.Heal, ["heal"]} to @command_routes in Commands.

## Constraints

- CLI must not reference UndercityCore types directly — only use what Gateway returns (item structs from check_inventory are already item maps, consistent with existing eat command).
- The self-target option in the target selector must be rendered distinctly (e.g. 'Yourself') so it reads naturally in the prompt.
- GameState.continue/3 must be called with updated new_ap and new_hp on success.
- On other-heal success, new_hp is the healer's HP (unchanged) — this comes from the server result shape.

## Acceptance criteria

- 'heal' opens target selector, then inventory selector; cancel at either returns state unchanged
- 'heal <target>' skips target selector, opens inventory selector
- 'heal <target> <n>' dispatches directly
- Self option appears in target selector for 'heal'
- Success message shown for self-heal and other-heal with correct item name and amount
- 'not_healable' error shows correct warning
- 'invalid_target' shows warning when target not in vicinity
- AP and HP updated in GameState after successful self-heal
- Commands.usage_hints/0 includes 'heal [target] [n]'

## Test cases (CLI, async: true, Mimic mocking)

Mirrors attack_test.exs structure:
- Bare 'heal': target selector cancel → continue
- Bare 'heal': target selector (self) → inventory selector cancel → continue
- Bare 'heal': full pipeline (self) → success message, updated state
- Bare 'heal': full pipeline (other) → success message, updated state
- 'heal <target>': inventory selector → success
- 'heal <target> <n>': direct dispatch → success
- not_healable → warning
- invalid_target → warning
- exhausted → standard message (via handle_action)
notes: Experts consulted: undercity-cli-expert, test-expert. Depends on: undercity-go7.2 (Gateway :heal clause must exist before CLI can call it). The self-target selector pattern is new — the implementation may choose to build a small helper or extend TargetSelector. Discuss approach with cli-expert before implementing.

title: Defender notification for incoming attacks
description:
Notify the defending player that they have been attacked. On hit: '<attacker> strikes you with <weapon> for <N> damage.' On collapse: '<attacker> strikes you with <weapon> for <N> damage. You collapse.' No notification on miss — take_damage is never called on a miss path.

Delivery is handled by the Player.Inbox channel built in w6m.

## Server changes

### Player.take_damage signature change
Current (apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/player.ex:93):
  take_damage(player_id, amount :: pos_integer())

New:
  take_damage(player_id, {attacker_name, weapon_name, damage})

Update the @spec and the GenServer.call tuple to match.

### Player.Server handle_call (apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/player/server.ex:139)
Current pattern: {:take_damage, amount}
New pattern:     {:take_damage, {attacker_name, weapon_name, damage}}

After applying damage and saving state, call:
  Player.Inbox.send_message(player_id, message)

Message format on hit:      '\#{attacker_name} strikes you with \#{weapon_name} for \#{damage} damage.'
Message format on collapse: '\#{attacker_name} strikes you with \#{weapon_name} for \#{damage} damage. You collapse.'
No inbox message if the player is already collapsed (early-return path unchanged).

Inbox API (apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/player/inbox.ex):
  Inbox.send_message(player_id, text) — cast, serialised write

## CLI changes

### Game loop (apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/game_loop.ex:19)
At the top of loop/2, before View.render_messages, pull inbox messages and push them into MessageBuffer:

  Gateway.messages_for(player.id)
  |> Enum.each(&MessageBuffer.push([&1]))

Gateway function: Gateway.messages_for/1 (apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/gateway.ex)
Returns: list of {text} tuples — unwrap before passing to MessageBuffer.push/1 which expects a list of strings.
MessageBuffer module: apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/message_buffer.ex

title: CLI: Commands.Use module
description:
Add a new 'use' command to the CLI supporting both self-targeted and player-targeted item use. Follows the attack command pattern for target selection and the eat command pattern for inventory selection.

## New files

## Changes
- apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/commands.ex — register {UndercityCli.Commands.Use, ["use"]} in @command_routes

## Command syntax
  use                      — open InventorySelector, apply to self
  use <n>                  — apply item at index n to self (0-based)
  use <n> on <player>      — apply item at index n to named player
  use on <player>          — open InventorySelector, apply to named player

## dispatch/5 and dispatch/6
Follows attack.ex pattern:
- dispatch/6 includes TargetSelector module for interactive target selection
- Self-targeting: target_id = player_id (no TargetSelector call)
- Other-targeting: look up name in state.vicinity.people via find_target_id/2, same as attack
- Gateway call: gateway.perform(player_id, block_id, :use_item, {target_id, item_index})

## Outcome handling
Success on self: message_buffer.success("You press the Bandages to the wound. They hold."), return GameState.continue(state, new_ap, new_hp)
Success on other: message_buffer.success("You press the Bandages against {target}'s wound."), return GameState.continue(state, new_ap, state.hp)

Messages (from content-writer):
  Self heal:    "You press the Bandages to the wound. They hold."
  Heal other:   "You press the Bandages against {target}'s wound."
  Full HP fail: "{target} isn't wounded. The Bandages are spent." / "You aren't wounded. The Bandages are spent."

Errors routed through Commands.handle_action/4 as usual (:exhausted, :collapsed, :not_in_block).

## Acceptance criteria
- Bare 'use': opens InventorySelector; cancelled → state unchanged
- Bare 'use': item selected, no target → heals self; success message, AP and HP updated in state
- 'use <n>': heals self directly; success message, AP and HP updated
- 'use <n> on <player>': target found → success message, AP updated (HP unchanged for caster)
- 'use <n> on <player>': target not in vicinity → warn "{target} is not here.", state unchanged
- Gateway {:error, :item_missing} → warn, state unchanged
- Gateway {:error, :target_collapsed} → warn, state unchanged
- Gateway {:error, :exhausted} → standard exhaustion message
- Gateway {:error, :collapsed} → standard collapsed message

Use UndercityCli.CommandCase (provides async: true and Mimic). Model after attack_test.exs.
Mock InventorySelector, TargetSelector, Gateway, MessageBuffer.

title: Design OTP process architecture
description: Plan the supervision tree and process model for the game world. Decide on: tile GenServers, player processes, zombie behavior loop, AP recharge mechanism, and how these processes interact. Reference mechanics from undercity-ovm.
notes:
## OTP Process Architecture (MVP)

### Scope
3x3 grid, player movement, tile viewing. No zombies, no AP, no inventory, no persistence.

### Supervision Tree
```
Application
├── TileRegistry        — Elixir Registry, tiles keyed by unique name (e.g. "town_square")
└── TileSupervisor
    ├── Tile("town_square") ... 9 tile GenServers
```

### Tile GenServer
- Holds: name, biome, description, list of player names currently present
- Looked up via Registry by tile name
- Handles: add_player, remove_player, get_state

### Grid / Adjacency
- Tiles have unique names, not coordinates
- Adjacency defined in a static map config (which tile connects to which)
- Movement validated against adjacency map

### CLI Client
- Holds its own current tile name locally
- On connect: spawns on a starting tile (e.g. "town_square"), tells tile to add player
- On move: calculates target from adjacency map, tells old tile to remove, new tile to add, updates local position
- On look: queries current tile + adjacent tiles via Registry
- On disconnect: tells current tile to remove player

### Future Additions (not in MVP)
- GameServer / player registry for persistence and reconnection
- Player processes for AP, inventory, HP
- ZombieManager process
- Player location lookup process for multi-node

title: Two-column CLI layout with persistent message log
description:
Rework the terminal layout into two columns:

Left column (~80%): holds everything currently rendered — surroundings grid, block description, selectors, and input prompt.

Right column (~20%): persistent scrolling message log. Messages accumulate rather than clearing each loop iteration. Once the column is full (max based roughly on the height of the left column), new messages push the oldest off the top. Behaves like a ring buffer — most recent messages always visible, history scrolls out.

This replaces the current flush-and-clear model in MessageBuffer / View.render_messages. MessageBuffer's push API stays the same but the render side changes to append rather than replace.

Key open questions before implementation:
- How does Owl.LiveScreen support multi-column layout? Check if a side-by-side panel arrangement is supported or if a custom approach is needed.
- What is the terminal width assumption (fixed 80 cols, or dynamic)?
- Max message count: derive from terminal height (e.g. rows - header rows) or a fixed constant?

Relevant files:
- apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/view.ex — View.init, render_messages
- apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/view/screen.ex — Owl.LiveScreen wrapper
- apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/message_buffer.ex — current flush model
- apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/game_loop.ex — sync_messages, loop

title: Server does not validate player location before dispatching actions
description:
## Problem

Gateway.perform/4 accepts a caller-supplied block_id and dispatches actions against it without verifying the player is actually in that block. A buggy or adversarial client could perform actions on blocks they are not standing in.

Movement is the exception — Actions.Movement does call Block.has_person? before allowing a leave. But search and scribble have no equivalent check:

```elixir
# Client supplies block_id — server trusts it
Gateway.perform(my_player_id, "some_block_across_the_map", :search, nil)
# ^ succeeds — player can loot a block they are not in

Gateway.perform(my_player_id, "some_block_across_the_map", :scribble, "hello")
# ^ succeeds — player can write on any wall in the world
```

## Possible Fix

Add a single location guard in Gateway before dispatching any perform call, rather than each action implementing its own check:

```elixir
def perform(player_id, block_id, action, args) do
  if Block.has_person?(block_id, player_id) do
    dispatch(player_id, block_id, action, args)
  else
    {:error, :not_in_block}
  end
end
```

This would cover all current and future actions uniformly, and Movement could drop its redundant has_person? check.

Discuss the right approach when the bead is started.
design:
## Implementation Plan

### 1. Refactor Gateway.perform/4
File: apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/gateway.ex

Replace the 4 per-action clauses with 2 public clauses + private dispatch:

  # Clause 1: eat — player-inventory-scoped, block_id irrelevant
  def perform(player_id, _block_id, :eat, index) do
    Actions.Eat.eat(player_id, index)
  end

  # Clause 2: everything else — validate location first
  def perform(player_id, block_id, action, args) do
    if Block.has_person?(block_id, player_id) do
      dispatch(player_id, block_id, action, args)
    else
      {:error, :not_in_block}
    end
  end

  defp dispatch(player_id, block_id, :move, direction), do: Actions.Movement.move(player_id, block_id, direction)
  defp dispatch(player_id, block_id, :search, _args), do: Actions.Search.search(player_id, block_id)
  defp dispatch(player_id, block_id, :scribble, text), do: Actions.Scribble.scribble(player_id, block_id, text)

Future combat actions go into dispatch/4 and get location validation for free.

### 2. Remove redundant check from Actions.Movement
File: apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/actions/movement.ex

Gateway now guarantees the player is in the block before move/3 is called.
Remove the Block.has_person? check and the false -> {:error, :not_found} clause from move/3.

### 3. Add :not_in_block handler to Commands
File: apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/commands.ex

Add a handle_action clause before the catch-all:

  def handle_action({:error, :not_in_block}, state, message_buffer, _callback) do
    message_buffer.warn("You can't do that from here.")
    GameState.continue(state)
  end

Without this, the error falls through to action-specific callbacks which don't handle it,
causing a crash.

### 4. Add tests
File: apps/undercity_server/test/undercity_server/gateway_test.exs

Add 2 test cases:
- search fails when player is not in the supplied block_id
- scribble fails when player is not in the supplied block_id

### Files
- apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/gateway.ex
- apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/actions/movement.ex
- apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/commands.ex
- apps/undercity_server/test/undercity_server/gateway_test.exs
notes: Step 2 (remove redundant Block.has_person? from Actions.Movement) is owned by this bead, not undercity-plw. The plw bead reorders movement but leaves the guard in place; mof removes it once Gateway provides the location check.

title: Fix flaky gateway test: entering with same name
description:
The 'enter/1 entering with the same name does not create a duplicate' test in gateway_test.exs fails intermittently due to stale DETS player data from previous test runs.

Root cause: Player.Server.handle_call(:constitution) calls Health.current(player.health) where health is nil. This occurs when a player is reloaded from DETS but the persisted data has a nil health field — indicating the record was written before the health field was added to the Player struct.

The unique_name() helper generates player_<rand 1..100000>, so there is a ~1/100000 chance per test run of colliding with stale DETS data that lacks a health field.

Error trace:
  (FunctionClauseError) no function clause matching in UndercityCore.Health.current/1
    UndercityCore.Health.current(nil)
    lib/undercity_server/player/server.ex:152 UndercityServer.Player.Server.handle_call/3
  Triggered by: GenServer.call(:player_2bd27f51eb066b0c, :constitution) in Session.enter/1

Possible fixes:
- Wipe test DETS between runs (mix undercity.reset before test suite)
- Guard Player.Server init to default health to Health.new() when nil
- Increase rand range in unique_name() to reduce collision chance

title: Design CLI client architecture and interaction model
description: Define how the CLI client works: how it starts, connects to the core, handles user input, renders output, and manages its local state. Builds on the OTP architecture from undercity-lta.
notes:
## CLI Client Architecture

### Connectivity
- Erlang distribution via named nodes (--sname) and shared cookie
- Server runs as a named BEAM node, CLI clients connect to it
- Multiple CLI clients can connect to the same server from separate terminals
- CLI calls tile processes on the server node directly via Registry (transparent cross-node calls)

### Commands (mix tasks)
- `mix undercity.server --name my_server` — starts the core, boots tiles/registry/supervisor, prints server info
- `mix undercity.join --server my_server --player lewis` — connects to server node, spawns player on starting tile, enters game loop
- Multiple players: each opens a new terminal and runs the join command

### CLI Client Behavior
- On join: connects to server node, prompts for player name (or passed via --player flag), spawns on starting tile, tells tile to add player
- Holds local state: current tile name
- Sends commands to tile GenServers on the server node via Registry
- On disconnect: tells current tile to remove player

### Future Improvements
- Move from mix tasks to escript for a standalone binary (`./undercity server`, `./undercity join`)
- Explore the named node approach further once MVP is working
- Server name/key typed by player on CLI startup to connect to a specific server

title: Eventually consistent movement with write-ahead log
description: Currently Gateway.move does leave then join as separate GenServer calls. If the process crashes between them, the player is lost from both blocks. Implement a write-ahead log (WAL) so movement is eventually consistent with no data loss or duplication. The WAL records the intent (player, from_block, to_block) before any state changes. A repair process periodically scans incomplete WAL entries and completes or rolls them back. This decouples the two-phase move into individually safe steps that can be recovered from any failure point.
notes:
Open design questions to resolve when picking this up:
- Where does the WAL live? Dedicated GenServer, DETS file, or something else? Per-block or global?
- What does a WAL entry lifecycle look like? (e.g. pending → leaving → joining → complete)
- How does the repair process detect incomplete entries — timeout-based? Heartbeat? On startup only?
- Does the move caller wait for full completion or return optimistically after writing the intent?

Blocked by 1gr (Searching) since we may want to consider making part of the move sequence tracking location in player state and moving away from needing to query every block when a client connects to get the player's position. Discuss more when working on this bead and split into a separate subtask if needed.
close_reason: After design discussion, the WAL approach was deemed unnecessary. The simpler leave → set_block → join ordering combined with DETS-based position restore on reconnect provides sufficient crash safety. Key properties: (1) player can never be in two blocks simultaneously since leave happens first, (2) DETS always holds either the source or destination block_id so position is never permanently lost, (3) on reconnect, if has_person? is false and the scan finds nobody, trust DETS and rejoin that block rather than falling back to spawn. This approach is folded into undercity-plw.

title: Add behaviours and Mox for injectable collaborators
description:
## Context

Currently, injectable collaborators (gateway, message_buffer, and the planned inventory_selector) are passed as module atoms and duck-typed — no formal interface/contract exists, and test doubles are plain modules in test/support/fakes.ex.

Mox (https://hex.pm/packages/mox) is the idiomatic Elixir answer for this. It requires defining @behaviour modules for each collaborator (like Java/Go interfaces), then uses Mox.defmock/2 to generate per-process mocks with expectations. Key benefits:
- Compiler warns if an implementing module is missing a declared callback
- Mocks are process-scoped so async: true tests stay safe
- Explicit contracts document what each collaborator must provide

## Suggested approach

1. Define @behaviour modules for gateway, message_buffer, and inventory_selector (and any others)
2. Add mox as a test-only dep in mix.exs
3. Replace the plain fake modules in test/support/fakes.ex with Mox-based mocks
4. Simplify command function signatures — instead of passing module atoms directly, the behaviours make the contract clear and Mox handles test doubles

## Note

In bead undercity-1ll, we chose explicit DI (5th positional arg) over Mox for the InventorySelector because Mox was not yet in the project and adding it just for one collaborator felt inconsistent. This bead would be the proper follow-up to retrofit everything consistently.
design:
## Implementation Plan

### Phase 1: Define @behaviour modules (3 new files)

apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/behaviour/gateway.ex
- Module: UndercityServer.Behaviour.Gateway
- Callbacks: perform/4, check_inventory/1, drop_item/2, connect/1, enter/1

apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/behaviour/message_buffer.ex
- Module: UndercityCli.Behaviour.MessageBuffer
- Callbacks: push/2, push/1, info/1, success/1, warn/1, flush/0

apps/undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/behaviour/inventory_selector.ex
- Module: UndercityCli.Behaviour.InventorySelector
- Callbacks: select/2 — returns {:ok, non_neg_integer()} | :cancel

### Phase 2: Add @behaviour to real implementations
- UndercityServer.Gateway — add @behaviour UndercityServer.Behaviour.Gateway
- UndercityCli.MessageBuffer — add @behaviour UndercityCli.Behaviour.MessageBuffer
- UndercityCli.View.InventorySelector — add @behaviour UndercityCli.Behaviour.InventorySelector

### Phase 3: Add Mox dependency
- apps/undercity_cli/mix.exs — add {:mox, "~> 1.0", only: :test}
- apps/undercity_server/mix.exs — add {:mox, "~> 1.0", only: :test}

### Phase 4: Test infrastructure
- Create apps/undercity_cli/test/support/mocks.exs — defmock for MockGateway, MockMessageBuffer, MockInventorySelector
- Create apps/undercity_server/test/support/mocks.exs — defmock for MockGateway
- Update both test_helper.exs files to load the mocks files

### Phase 5: Annotate existing fakes
- test/support/fakes.ex — add @behaviour declarations to FakeGateway, FakeMessageBuffer, SelectFirstSelector, CancelSelector
- This validates fakes against the contracts at compile time while keeping them as a migration reference

### Phase 6: Migrate command tests to Mox
Files to migrate:
- test/undercity_cli/commands/drop_test.exs
- test/undercity_cli/commands/eat_test.exs
- test/undercity_cli/commands/search_test.exs
- test/undercity_cli/commands/move_test.exs
- test/undercity_cli/commands/scribble_test.exs
- test/undercity_cli/commands/inventory_test.exs

Pattern: replace inline test double modules and fake atoms with Mox.expect/3 calls.
Add Mox.verify_on_exit\!(self()) in each test's setup — keeps async: true safe.

Example migration:
  # Before
  assert {:continue, state} = Drop.dispatch("drop", @state, FakeGateway, FakeMessageBuffer, SelectFirstSelector)

  # After (in setup: Mox.verify_on_exit\!(self()))
  expect(MockGateway, :drop_item, fn "player1", 0 -> {:ok, "Sword", 9} end)
  expect(MockMessageBuffer, :info, fn _ -> :ok end)
  expect(MockInventorySelector, :select, fn _items, _ -> {:ok, 0} end)
  assert {:continue, state} = Drop.dispatch("drop", @state, MockGateway, MockMessageBuffer, MockInventorySelector)

Once all tests are migrated, fakes.ex can be deleted.

### Files Summary
New:   undercity_server/lib/.../behaviour/gateway.ex
New:   undercity_cli/lib/.../behaviour/message_buffer.ex
New:   undercity_cli/lib/.../behaviour/inventory_selector.ex
New:   undercity_cli/test/support/mocks.exs
New:   undercity_server/test/support/mocks.exs
Mod:   undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/gateway.ex
Mod:   undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/message_buffer.ex
Mod:   undercity_cli/lib/undercity_cli/view/inventory_selector.ex
Mod:   undercity_cli/mix.exs
Mod:   undercity_server/mix.exs
Mod:   undercity_cli/test/support/fakes.ex
Mod:   undercity_cli/test/test_helper.exs
Mod:   undercity_server/test/test_helper.exs
Mod:   6x command test files
Del:   undercity_cli/test/support/fakes.ex (once migration complete)

title: Core: Remedy module and healing item domain logic
description:
Introduce the domain-layer support for a single-use healing item.

## What to build

1. Create UndercityCore.Remedy module (mirrors Food/Weapon pattern):
   - A compile-time @registry map of item name => heal amount (integer HP restored)
   - remedy?(name) :: boolean - true if the item name is a registered remedy
   - effect(name) :: {:heal, pos_integer()} | :not_a_remedy

2. Add UndercityCore.Player.use_remedy/3 (player, index, now):
   - Same structure as Player.eat/3
   - Validates index, checks Remedy.remedy?(item.name), calls exert/3 (1 AP cost), applies Health.apply_effect with {:heal, amount}, removes item from inventory
   - Returns {:ok, player, item, {:heal, amount}} | {:error, :invalid_index} | {:error, :not_a_remedy, item_name} | {:error, :exhausted} | {:error, :collapsed}

3. Add UndercityCore.Player.receive_heal/2 (player, amount):
   - Pure function: applies Health.apply_effect(player.health, {:heal, amount}), returns updated player
   - No AP cost, no collapse check (collapsed players can be healed)
   - Used when another player heals you

4. Register the healing item name in LootTable (at least one block type, e.g. graveyard or inn).

## Constraints

- Item.new(name, 1) already works for single-use items; no changes needed to Item.
- Health.apply_effect already clamps to max HP — no extra clamping needed.
- The item name must match whatever content-writer decides; coordinate before finalising registry entry.
- All functions must be pure (no processes).

## Acceptance criteria

- Remedy.remedy?(healing_item_name) returns true; Remedy.remedy?("Mushroom") returns false
- Remedy.effect(healing_item_name) returns {:heal, N} for some N > 0
- Player.use_remedy/3 with a valid remedy index returns {:ok, player, item, {:heal, N}} and HP is increased (capped at max)
- Player.use_remedy/3 on a non-remedy item returns {:error, :not_a_remedy, name}
- Player.use_remedy/3 at max HP still succeeds (no wasted AP edge case is a design choice, not an error)
- Player.receive_heal/2 increases HP up to Health.max(); never exceeds cap
- Player.receive_heal/2 works when player HP is 0 (collapsed — healing a downed ally is valid)
- LootTable includes the item with an appropriate probability for at least one block type
- All tests async: true, no processes
close_reason: Deleting to re-plan with improved ticket-planner agent

title: Weapon item properties and lookup
description: Extend the item system to support weapon properties. Options: 1) Simple approach: a weapon registry/map that maps item names to {damage_min, damage_max, hit_modifier} stats. The Item struct stays unchanged, combat logic looks up stats by name. 2) Richer approach: extend Item struct with optional weapon fields. Recommend option 1 for simplicity. Also need a way to determine if a player has a weapon — scan inventory for any item whose name is in the weapon registry.

title: Define core game mechanics and requirements
description: Document the initial game design: action point system (how many, restock rate, what spends them), core gameplay loop, what a player can do per turn, win/loss conditions if any, and the general theme/setting of undercity. Output: a requirements doc or design notes we can reference.
notes:
## Core Game Mechanics

### World / Map
- Fixed 10x10 grid, expandable later
- Each tile has a biome (field, road, river, ruins, forest, village, etc.)
- Each tile has one structure/feature (building, fountain, well, shrine, etc.) — interactable
- Players can modify structures (barricade, graffiti, loot) but not build new ones
- Modifications persist for all players, may decay over time (TBD)
- 9-tile visibility bubble (current tile + 8 adjacent)
- Multiple players per tile — see names on your tile and adjacent tiles
- Can target other players for actions (attack, heal, etc.)
- Zombies occupy tiles like players, roam via a slow background behavior loop (TBD)

### Actions & AP System
- All actions have an AP cost (variable per action, tunable)
- Moving costs AP — traversal is a meaningful decision
- Some actions may be free (viewing, reading — TBD)
- AP recharges on a time-based drip, no other sources initially
- Exact numbers (pool size, recharge rate, action costs) left flexible for tuning
- Target pacing: ~30 min daily session

### Player State
- HP pool (values TBD)
- Permadeath — dead players become AI zombies on their death tile
- No hunger/thirst for now
- Inventory with limited slots (count TBD)
- No skills/abilities initially, future addition

### Items & Economy
- Item types: weapons, armor/clothing, medical, crafting materials, consumables
- No durability initially
- Player-to-player trading only — simple give/receive
- Loot tables per tile biome
- Structures have their own loot tables
- No formal currency — barter/emergent economy

### Combat
- Action-based: use [weapon] on [target], costs AP
- PvP always on, same tile required
- PvE identical mechanically
- Resolution: % to hit > % to save > damage (Warhammer-style)
- Weapons have stats (hit chance, damage)
- Instant permadeath, dead player becomes AI zombie

title: Normalize Gateway public API return types
description:
Gateway's public API has inconsistent return types across its methods. Consider normalizing to a consistent pattern ({:ok, result} / {:error, reason}) or deliberately choosing where variation is justified.

Current state:
- move → {:ok, vicinity} / {:error, reason}
- search → {:found, item} / :nothing
- scribble → :ok / {:error, reason}
- get_inventory → items directly
- connect → {:ok, {player_id, vicinity}} / {:error, reason}
- enter → {player_id, vicinity} (unwrapped, called inside connect)

Questions to answer:
- Should everything use {:ok, result} / {:error, reason}?
- Is some variation justified for expressiveness (e.g. search returning :nothing)?
- How does this interact with the Gateway facade pattern (should Gateway normalize even if action modules return varied types)?
close_reason: Variation in return types is deliberate and justified. Each return type is shaped by consumer needs and domain semantics. Forcing {:ok, _}/{:error, _} everywhere would lose expressiveness and fight OTP let-it-crash conventions.

title: Refactor Gateway and organise server app structure
description:
Gateway is currently ~190 lines with 5 public functions acting as the single client entry point to the server. As more actions are added (combat, trading, crafting, dialogue, etc.), it will grow well beyond a manageable size. This bead covers:

1. **Define architectural goals** — What growth are we planning to support? What kinds of actions/features will clients need? How does the proposed structure accommodate that growth?

2. **Gateway refactoring** — Decide on a decomposition strategy. Current candidates:
   - Split Gateway into domain-focused sub-modules (e.g. Gateway.Movement, Gateway.Inventory, Gateway.Interaction) with Gateway as a thin facade or removed entirely
   - Normalize return types across the public API ({:ok, result} / {:error, reason} consistently)

3. **Server app organisation** — The flat UndercityServer namespace has two implicit subsystems (Player and Block) that could benefit from explicit namespacing:
   - Player subsystem: Player, PlayerIdentity, PlayerStore, PlayerSupervisor
   - Block subsystem: Block, BlockSupervisor, Store
   - Store naming is ambiguous (block-specific but named generically)

4. **Decide what stays** — Some things are fine as-is (the block_call/player_call helpers, server_node threading). The plan should distinguish real structural improvements from churn.

title: Eliminate linear scan in find_player_block
description:
Session.find_player_block/1 locates a player's current block by iterating over every block in WorldMap.blocks() and calling Block.has_person? on each one until it finds a match. This is O(n) in the number of blocks.

Currently the block count is small enough that this works, but we want to support ~10k blocks in the future, at which point a linear scan with a GenServer.call per block becomes a serious bottleneck — both in latency (sequential calls) and in load on block processes (every reconnect touches every block).

This is called during enter/1 in Session when a returning player reconnects and we need to find which block they're in. It falls back to the spawn block if no match is found.

The solution should track player-to-block mappings so lookup is O(1). The specific mechanism (player process state, a dedicated index, PlayerStore metadata, etc.) should be decided when this is picked up, taking into account the server's existing architecture and the ~10k block target.
design:
## Implementation Plan

### Approach
Store block_id in Player GenServer state, persisted to DETS via PlayerStore. This makes lookup O(1) on reconnect with a fallback for stale/missing values.

### Movement Ordering
The move sequence must follow this order to prevent inconsistent state:

  1. Block.leave(from_block)     -- remove from source first
  2. Player.set_block(dest)      -- update DETS with new position
  3. Block.join(dest)            -- add to destination

This ordering guarantees the player can never be in two blocks simultaneously.
The only crash window leaves the player in neither block, which is handled by reconnect restore.

### New Player API
- Player.set_block(player_id, block_id) — new public function + handle_call callback
- Player.get_block(player_id) — new public function + handle_call callback
- Add block_id: nil to Player init state (backward compat: Map.get with nil default for old DETS records)

### Write points for block_id
1. New player initial spawn (Session.enter/1) — after Block.join to spawn block
2. Movement (Actions.Movement.move/3) — step 2 in the ordered sequence above

### Fast path on reconnect (Session.find_player_block/1)
1. Player.get_block(player_id) → cached block_id from DETS
2. If block_id is nil → player has never moved, must be at spawn. Call Block.join(spawn) + Player.set_block(spawn) and return spawn_block.
3. Block.has_person?(block_id, player_id) → verify player is actually there
4. If found → use that block ✓
5. If not found → full scan across all blocks
6. If scan finds block → return that block ✓
7. If scan finds nothing → trust DETS: call Block.join(block_id) to restore position, return block_id ✓

Note: Old DETS records (nil block_id) are handled by resetting via the mix reset task, not by migration logic. The nil path above only applies to newly created players who have not yet moved.

### Crash recovery properties
- Crash after leave, before set_block: DETS has source → restored to source ✓
- Crash after set_block, before join: DETS has dest → restored to dest ✓
- No scenario where position is permanently lost (assuming DETS integrity)

### Remove redundant check from Actions.Movement
The existing Block.has_person? check in move/3 is left in place until undercity-mof adds its own location guard in Gateway.

### Files
- apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/player.ex — add block_id field, set_block/2, get_block/1
- apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/session.ex — update find_player_block/1 with fast path; call Player.set_block after spawn join and after find_player_block on reconnect
- apps/undercity_server/lib/undercity_server/actions/movement.ex — reorder to leave → set_block → join

title: Content: name and flavour text for healing item
description:
Decide the in-world name and player-facing messages for the single-use healing consumable.

## What to decide

1. Item name — must feel consistent with the world (gritty medieval fantasy, post-apocalyptic decay). Existing items for reference: Mushroom, Iron Pipe, Chalk, Junk. The name will be registered in UndercityCore.Remedy and LootTable, so it must be finalised before those modules are written.

2. Heal amount — how many HP does one use restore? Health max is 50. Existing food comparison: Mushroom heals 5 HP (but also risks 5 damage). A dedicated healing item should be reliably beneficial but not overpowered. Suggest a value in the 8-15 HP range.

3. Player-facing messages for:
   a. Self-heal success: e.g. 'You apply the [item] and recover N HP.'
   b. Heal-other success (user side): e.g. 'You apply the [item] to [target] and restore N HP.'
   c. Heal-other success (target side — shown in their message buffer): e.g. '[user] applies a [item] to your wounds.'
   d. No valid remedy in inventory: e.g. 'You have nothing to treat wounds with.'
   e. Target not found (invalid target name): e.g. 'There is no one here by that name.'

4. Loot table placement — which block types should yield this item, and at what probability? Consider rarity vs. utility.

## Constraints

- Refer to STYLE_GUIDE.md: gritty, sensory, grounded in decay. Berserk / Dark Souls tone.
- Do not name the world directly in flavour text.
- Messages should be concise — one sentence.
- The item name must be agreed before the Core bead is implemented.

title: Investigate cross-node notification delivery mechanism
description:
Investigate and decide on the mechanism for delivering notifications from the server node to CLI session processes on a different Erlang node. This is needed because the attacker's combat action runs on the server node but the defender's CLI session lives on the CLI node — we need a way to bridge that gap.

Context:
- CLI and server run on separate distributed Erlang nodes (CLI uses via/1 tuples with UndercityServer.server_node() for remote GenServer calls)
- All current communication is CLI-initiated synchronous GenServer.call — there is no async server-to-CLI push
- Registry is node-local so cannot directly deliver cross-node messages
- LiveScreen can render updates async (no need for player input to trigger display)
- The flash buffer (eyy) will handle message accumulation/display on the CLI side

Options to evaluate:
1. Phoenix.PubSub — designed for distributed pub/sub, but adds a new dependency
2. Server-side inbox GenServer — accumulates messages on server node, CLI polls periodically or on each loop iteration (fits existing call-into-server pattern)
3. Direct process messaging — CLI process registers globally (e.g. :global or pg), server sends messages directly via send/2
4. Hybrid — any combination worth considering

Decision criteria:
- Must work across distributed Erlang nodes
- Should be simple and fit existing architecture patterns
- Consider future uses beyond combat: room chat, player enter/leave notifications, ambient events
- Minimal new dependencies preferred
- Consider whether CLI should poll or subscribe

Deliverable: A written decision in the bead notes field documenting the chosen approach with rationale.
notes: Decision: Server-side Inbox + CLI polling (Option 2). Zero new dependencies, fits existing loop pattern perfectly. Key design: (1) Inbox accumulates messages while player is offline; clears on fetch — reconnecting player gets everything that accumulated while away. (2) MessageBuffer owns the pull — calls Gateway.fetch_inbox(player_id) internally rather than the game loop explicitly fetching. (3) Inbox capped at 50 messages using a ring buffer — when full, oldest message is cycled out on arrival of a new one.
close_reason: Decision: server-side Inbox GenServer, clears on fetch by MessageBuffer. 50-message ring buffer cap, message loss acceptable.

title: Write 3x3 map content (tile names, biomes, descriptions)
description: Author the actual game content for the 9 tiles in the 3x3 grid: unique names, biomes, and flavour text descriptions. Setting is a dark medieval zombie apocalypse (tone of Berserk / Between Two Fires — grim, grounded, not heroic). Biome types include field, road, river, ruins, forest, village, etc. Each tile should feel distinct. This is the creative/data side that feeds into the WorldMap config module.

title: Wire combat into server layer and Player GenServer
description:
Server-side wiring for combat in undercity_server. Connects the core combat resolution (26g) to the OTP layer.

Requires:
1) Player.take_damage(player_id, amount) — new public API on the Player GenServer. Calls Health.apply_effect({:damage, amount}) on the player's health state. Returns {:ok, new_hp} or similar. Must handle the case where target is already at 0 HP.
2) Actions.Combat module in undercity_server — orchestrates a combat action: looks up attacker's weapon (via weapon registry from ome), calls core combat resolution (26g) for hit/miss/damage, applies damage to target via Player.take_damage, returns a result tuple for message rendering (e.g. {:hit, target_name, damage, new_hp} | {:miss, target_name} | {:collapsed, target_name, damage}).
3) Gateway.perform(player_id, block_id, :attack, target_id) clause routing to Actions.Combat.
4) AP cost integration — attack action should cost AP via Player.perform/3 wrapper.
notes:
Gateway contract: perform(player_id, block_id, :attack, {target_id, weapon_index}). Validate weapon before spending AP (like eat's :not_edible check) — invalid_weapon should return before Player.perform/3 is called.

Return shape expected by Commands.Attack:
- {:ok, {:hit, target_name, weapon_name, damage}, new_ap}
- {:ok, {:collapsed, target_name, weapon_name, damage}, new_ap}
- {:ok, {:miss, target_name}, new_ap}
- {:error, :invalid_weapon} — item at weapon_index is not in weapon registry
- {:error, :exhausted} / {:error, :collapsed} — from Player.perform/3

Also move these validations server-side (currently only in CLI):
- Target not in block (target_id not in block's people set)
- Attacker targeting themselves (player_id == target_id)
Both should return appropriate error tuples so the server is the authority on world state.

title: Create cool connecting spinner for CLI
description: Add a visually appealing animated spinner to display while the CLI is connecting to the server. Inspired by Claude Code's TUI aesthetics - should feel polished and 'swaggy'.
design:
## Design: Smooth Braille + Color Pulse + Dynamic Text

### Visual Elements
1. **Braille dot spinner** (⠋⠙⠹⠸⠼⠴⠦⠧⠇⠏) - smooth, modern feel
2. **Color cycling** - magenta → cyan → blue → magenta (neon underground vibe)
3. **Thematic status messages** that rotate:
   - Establishing connection...
   - Descending into the undercity...
   - Tunneling through...
   - Emerging... (on success)
4. **Retry feedback** - show attempt number subtly on retries

### States
- **Success:** ✓ Connected to [server] as [player] (green/cyan)
- **Failure:** ✗ Could not reach the undercity (red/magenta)

### Architecture
- Create Spinner module in undercity_cli
- Runs in separate process (animates during connection retries)
- Uses Bunt for ANSI colors
- Simple API: Spinner.start/1, Spinner.update/1, Spinner.stop/1
close_reason: Implemented rotating cross spinner with gothic themed messages, smooth 24-bit text color pulsing, and muted color palette

title: Simplify failure message

title: Implement selector for indexed commands
description: Add a selector UI for commands that submit an index (drop item, eat). Instead of typing the full command with args, the user types the command alone, which opens a selector showing available options. Selecting an option performs the command with the index of the selected item.
notes:
## Implementation

Selector is implemented across four files:

- **`game_loop.ex`** — `parse("drop")` → `:select_drop` and `parse("eat")` → `:select_eat` added before the existing indexed-arg clauses. Typed `drop 1` / `eat 2` still works unchanged.
- **`commands.ex`** — `dispatch(:select_drop, ...)` and `dispatch(:select_eat, ...)` fetch inventory via `Gateway.check_inventory`, warn if empty, open the selector, then delegate to the existing `{:drop, index}` / `{:eat, index}` dispatch on selection. Help text updated from `drop <n>` to `drop [n]`.
- **`view/screen.ex`** — Added a `:selector` LiveScreen block (renders pre-built Owl.Data or nil → "") and `update_selector/1`.
- **`view/inventory_selector.ex`** — `select/2` pushes the rendered list into the `:selector` block, reads input in a loop, then sets the block back to nil — clearing the UI in-place on both cancel and selection. Uses `IO.ANSI.color(103)` (same as block name highlight in BlockDescription) for numbers and label. A leading `"\n"` in the render provides a separator above the list. `parse_choice/2` is public (`@doc false`) for testability.

## Design decisions
- All inventory items shown for both drop and eat (generic selector, server rejects non-edible items with a warning as before).
- Cancel option appended as last entry; selecting it returns `:cancel` and clears the UI without dispatching.
- On invalid input the selector stays visible and re-prompts silently (no error message).
- Selector block is registered in `Screen.init/0` alongside other LiveScreen blocks, so it is always available and initially empty.

## Status
Implementation complete, pending user review before commit.

title: Server-side player notification channel
description: Implement a mechanism for the server to push messages to a specific player's CLI session asynchronously. Uses a single Player.Inbox GenServer owning an ETS table. Writes are serialised through the GenServer; reads use :ets.take/2 directly for atomic fetch-and-clear with no bottleneck. Player.Server.handle_call for take_damage is extended to accept a source tuple {attacker_name, weapon_name, damage} and queue an inbox message after applying damage.
design:
Single Player.Inbox GenServer started at boot under the application supervisor. Owns an ETS table keyed by player_id. Messages are stored as {player_id, [messages]} where each message is a {text} tuple (newest-first, prepended to head on insert).

API:
- Inbox.send_message(player_id, text) — GenServer cast; prepends message to player's list in ETS. Serialises all writes through the GenServer to avoid concurrent append races.
- Inbox.fetch(player_id, n \ 50) — plain function, no GenServer hop; calls :ets.take/2 directly for atomic fetch-and-clear. Returns newest N messages. Callers access ETS directly for reads — no bottleneck.

No persistence (ETS only) — messages are lost on server restart. This is acceptable: offline players won't notice missed notifications, same as a crash.

No per-player process — one global GenServer owns the table. No lifecycle questions.

take_damage change:
- Player.take_damage/2 signature changes to take_damage(player_id, {attacker_name, weapon_name, damage})
- Player.Server handle_call pattern becomes {:take_damage, {attacker_name, weapon_name, damage}}
- After applying damage and saving, Player.Server calls Inbox.send_message with a composed message
- Message format on hit: '<attacker> strikes you with <weapon> for <N> damage.'
- Message format on collapse: '<attacker> strikes you with <weapon> for <N> damage. You collapse.'
- No inbox message if player is already collapsed (early return path unchanged)

Gateway:
- Gateway.fetch_inbox(player_id) — delegates to Inbox.fetch/1

CLI game loop:
- Each iteration calls Gateway.fetch_inbox(player_id), feeds results into MessageBuffer before flush
notes: Future direction: once inbox is established, route ALL server messages through it (command results, move descriptions, search finds, etc.) rather than returning them inline from Gateway calls. CLI simplifies to: start MessageBuffer, loop = pull inbox → render → read input → dispatch → repeat. Removes CLI-side message accumulation entirely — MessageBuffer just owns the pull-and-render cycle.

title: Define client-core interface/API
description: Design the boundary between the core game engine and client frontends. Determine how the CLI (and later web) client communicates with the core — direct function calls, message passing, or network protocol. This shapes the umbrella app structure.
notes:
## Client-Core Interface Decision

### Architecture
- Elixir umbrella project with separate apps: undercity_core and undercity_cli
- undercity_cli depends on undercity_core, never the reverse
- Core exposes a public API facade module — the boundary clients call into
- Internals (GenServers, processes) are hidden behind the facade

### Phase 1 (CLI)
- Direct function calls via the facade module (Option A)
- CLI and core run in the same BEAM node
- Simplest to start, no network overhead

### Phase 2 (multi-client / persistence)
- Move to message passing (Option B)
- Core runs as a standalone server node
- CLI clients connect to the running node from separate terminals
- Multiple simultaneous players via separate terminal sessions
- Public API surface stays conceptually the same

### Deferred
- Concrete API actions to be defined in MVP requirements task
- Network protocol (Option C) not needed — BEAM distribution handles node-to-node comms

title: Replace Owl with Ratatouille for terminal rendering
description:
## Goal

Replace the current Owl.LiveScreen-based rendering stack with Ratatouille, a declarative terminal UI framework using the Elm Architecture (TEA). This unblocks proper two-column layout (lz3) and gives us a responsive, composable view system.

## Why Replace Owl

Owl.LiveScreen blocks are strictly vertical and stacked — no side-by-side layout is possible. Achieving two-column layout in Owl requires manually composing iodata (zipping lines from left and right panels) into a single block, which is fragile and not responsive to terminal width.

Ratatouille provides a 12-column grid DSL (row/column, like HTML) where multi-column layout is first-class. Column widths are computed from actual terminal width at render time, so layouts are responsive. Two-column layout becomes:

  row do
    column(size: 9) do ... end
    column(size: 3) do ... end
  end

Two-column layout itself is OUT OF SCOPE for this bead — that is tracked in lz3, which is blocked by this one.

## Ratatouille Architecture

Implements the Elm Architecture (TEA) via three callbacks on a module that declares @behaviour Ratatouille.App:

- init/1 — receives runtime context (including window.height, window.width) and returns initial model
- update/2 — receives (model, msg) and returns new model. Messages come from: terminal events (keypresses, resize, mouse), subscription ticks, completed async commands
- render/1 — receives current model, returns a view tree rooted at a view element. Runtime calls this on each repaint.

The runtime is an OTP process that owns the render cycle (default 500ms interval). It uses ex_termbox (C NIF wrapping the termbox library) to take exclusive control of the terminal in raw mode via an alternate screen buffer.

## DSL Elements

view, row, column, panel, label, text, table, table_row, table_cell, bar, overlay, canvas, chart, tree, tree_node, sparkline

Layout example from rendering.exs:
  view(top_bar: top_bar, bottom_bar: bottom_bar) do
    panel title: 'Main', height: :fill do
      row do
        column(size: 4) do
          panel title: 'Left' do
            label(content: 'hello')
          end
        end
        column(size: 8) do
          panel title: 'Right' do
            label(content: 'world')
          end
        end
      end
    end
  end

## Async Patterns

Commands — Command.new(&fn/0, tag) runs a function async; result arrives as {tag, result} in update/2. Maps to our Gateway RPC calls (currently blocking sync_messages).

Subscriptions — Subscription.interval(ms, :message_name) delivers ticks to update/2. Maps to our sync_messages poll.

## Input Model

IMPORTANT CHANGE: Ratatouille is fully event-driven. There is no IO.gets. Every keypress arrives as {:event, %{ch: ch, key: key}} in update/2. Text input must be accumulated character by character in the model (editor.exs pattern). Enter triggers dispatch.

## Transferability

### Clean mappings
- GameState struct → TEA model (near 1:1, no change needed)
- Commands routing + modules → mostly reusable; return updated model instead of pushing to MessageBuffer
- MessageBuffer → becomes a messages: [] list field in the model; no Agent needed at all (simpler)
- View.BlockDescription, View.Status, View.Constitution → pure formatting logic is reusable; output format changes from Owl-tagged iodata to Ratatouille label/text DSL elements
- View.Surroundings 3x3 grid → rework from Owl.Table to Ratatouille table/panel DSL; hardcoded @cell_width 30 (~94 cols) goes away, widths become responsive

### Significant rework
- Input module (IO.gets) → DELETED entirely; replaced by keypress accumulation in model state (see editor.exs example)
- GameLoop → DELETED; replaced by TEA update/2 callback responding to events
- InventorySelector / TargetSelector → major rework; current blocking-receive pattern is incompatible; must become explicit model mode (e.g. model.mode = {:selecting_inventory, options}) with render/1 branching on mode and update/2 handling keypresses differently per mode
- Commands with multi-step IO (e.g. Scribble) → any IO.gets inside command modules must become model state transitions

### Terminal styling
Owl uses tagged iodata (Owl.Data.tag/2) and IO.ANSI for colours. Ratatouille uses keyword attributes on DSL elements: label(content: '...', color: :red, attributes: [:bold]). All colour/style code needs updating.

## Dependency

Ratatouille: ~> 0.5.0
Pulls in: ex_termbox (C NIF), elixir_make, asciichart

## Platform Note — CONFIRM BEFORE STARTING

ex_termbox uses a C NIF requiring POSIX terminal APIs (termios, tcsetattr for raw mode). This will NOT compile in MSYS2/Git Bash on Windows. Must be compiled and run from a POSIX environment (WSL2 recommended).

BEFORE BEGINNING IMPLEMENTATION: verify that mix compile succeeds with {:ratatouille, '~> 0.5.0'} added to deps, and that the rendering.exs example from the Ratatouille repo renders correctly in the target terminal.

title: Simplify CLI to Server GenServer call chain for actions
description: Investigate and reduce the number of GenServer calls needed for actions like move. Simplify the CLI <-> Server call chain to be more efficient.
design:
## Approach: Remove GameServer bottleneck, direct client-to-block communication

### Current Architecture
- CLI (client node) makes a single GenServer.call to GameServer (server node, globally registered)
- GameServer delegates to Gateway, which runs inside GameServer's process
- Gateway orchestrates multiple Block GenServer calls (find_person, leave, join, info)
- Block GenServers registered via local Registry on server node
- All player actions serialized through the single GameServer process

### Proposed Architecture
- **Drop GameServer** entirely — no central bottleneck
- **Block GenServers use local names** (e.g. :'block_\#{id}') instead of Registry
- **Gateway moves to the client side** — becomes the public API / client SDK
- Gateway runs in the caller's process and routes to Block GenServers on the server node using {name, node} tuples
- CLI calls Gateway directly as function calls, no GenServer.call to a middleman

### How it works
1. Client connects to server node (Node.connect/1)
2. Client calls Gateway.move(player, direction, block_id) — runs in client's process
3. Gateway makes GenServer.call({:'block_\#{id}', server_node}, msg) for each step
4. OTP routes the message to the named process on the server node
5. Block GenServer handles the action and replies back over the connection

### Trade-offs
- Multiple cross-node round-trips per action (3-4 for a move) instead of 1 — acceptable since the goal is eventual consistency
- Local atom names scale fine for the current world and up to ~10k blocks (under 1% of BEAM atom table)
- At 100k+ blocks, would revisit naming (Registry/string keys), but architecture will likely look different by then anyway
- Gateway API stays the same regardless of underlying naming scheme, so swappable later
notes: This change also removes the need for :global registration entirely. The only thing using :global is GameServer — once it's removed, all cross-node communication uses {local_name, node} tuples which are built-in OTP.
